claims.claim: When charging at 0.5C and discharging at 3C, the battery temperature rise after five cycles is higher than at 0.5C, 1C, and 2C discharge by 30.8°C, 21.4°C, and 9°C respectively.
claims.quote: the temperature rise ratio of the battery after the 3C cycle is 0.5C, 1C, 2C is higher by 30.8 °C, 21.4 °C, and 9 °C

claims.claim: When cycling a 18650 lithium-ion battery at a 3C charge and 3C discharge rate, the battery voltage suddenly drops to 0 V after the 2nd cycle, indicating battery failure.
claims.quote: when the battery is cycled at a 3C charging‐discharging rate, the battery voltage suddenly changes to 0 V after the 2nd cycle

claims.claim: For 0.5C charge with 2C discharge versus 2C charge with 0.5C discharge over 5 cycles, temperature increased 27°C and 17.2°C respectively, showing low-rate charge with high-rate discharge generates more heat when currents are relatively small.
claims.quote: The temperature of 0.5C charging with 2C discharge cycle and the 2C charge with 0.5C discharge cycle after 5 cycles have increased 27 °C and 17.2 °C, respectively.

claims.claim: For 2C charge with 3C discharge versus 3C charge with 2C discharge over 5 cycles, temperature increased 61.6°C and 63.5°C respectively, with the high-rate charging low-rate discharge cycle generating slightly more heat, which is the opposite trend from lower current pairs.
claims.quote: The temperature of 2C charging with 3C discharge cycle and the 3C charge with 2C discharge cycle after 5 cycles have increased 61.6 °C and 63.5 °C, respectively...Contrary to the results obtained from the previous two groups, the heat generated by the high ratio charging with low rate discharge cycle is larger.

claims.claim: At high discharge rates (>5C), an important part of battery heat generation is from decomposition of the SEI film, and as cycling progresses the SEI on the negative electrode thickens, continuously increasing heat generated by the SEI film.
claims.quote: during high‐rate (>5C) discharges, an important part of the battery's heat generation is the heat generated by the decomposition of the SEI (solid electrolyte interphase) film. As the cycle progresses, the SEI of the negative electrode is also thickened, and the heat generated by the SEI film is also continuously increased.

claims.claim: Under 1C discharge with charging rates of 0.5C, 1C, 2C, and 3C over five cycles, temperature increases were 13.8°C, 18.1°C, 37.6°C, and 44.3°C respectively, with heating rates of 0.18, 0.36, 0.50, and 1.11°C/min.
claims.quote: After five cycles, the cell temperature increased by 13.8 °C、18.1 °C、37.6 °C、44.3 °C, respectively. The heating rates of the batteries were 0.18 °C/min, 0.36 °C/min, 0.50 °C/min and 1.11 °C/min, respectively.

claims.claim: In a 4-cell series-connected Li-ion battery pack with 2mm cell spacing charged at 2C, all four batteries experienced thermal runaway, with a maximum temperature of 437°C and the first thermal runaway occurring at 2535 seconds at 231°C.
claims.quote: In this set of experiments, all the four batteries had thermal runaway, the No. 2 battery firstly had thermal runaway at 2535 seconds, and the thermal runaway temperature was 231°C. The maximum temperature of the thermal runaway is 437°C.

claims.claim: As cell spacing in a Li-ion battery pack increases from 2mm to 60mm under 2C charging, the number of batteries experiencing thermal runaway decreases from 4 to 1, and the maximum thermal runaway temperature decreases from 437°C to 405°C.
claims.quote: with the increase of the spacing of the single cells in the Li-ion battery packs, the starting time of the thermal runaway of the Li-ion battery packs also increases, the number of thermally runaway batteries decreases, and the maximum temperature of the thermal runaway decreases.

claims.claim: Increasing cell spacing reduces inter-cell heat transfer by shifting the dominant heat transfer mechanism from conduction to radiation, and larger spacing reduces the radiation area A1 between batteries, thereby reducing heat propagation through the pack.
claims.quote: When the pitch of the single cells in the Li-ion battery packs becomes larger, the radiation area A1 between batteries is reduced, which causes a reduction in the amount of heat that travels through the battery pack.

claims.claim: As the number of battery cells connected in series between the outlet and access points increases from 1 to 3, the thermal runaway start time decreases from 2455s to 2220s and the maximum thermal runaway temperature increases from 568°C to 692°C, with all 15 batteries experiencing thermal runaway at the highest series count.
claims.quote: as the number of battery cells connected in series between the access points increases, the thermal runaway start time decreases, and the initial temperature of the thermal runaway of the battery pack decreases.

claims.claim: Increasing the length of copper connecting strips between outlet and access points increases external circuit resistance, which at the same charging current increases heat generation and thereby decreases the thermal runaway start time.
claims.quote: the increase of the number of series battery packs between the outgoing point and the access point caused the increase of the distance between the access points at the outgoing point. Most of the heat comes from the heat released by the external copper used to connect the battery during the charging process. When the copper length increases, the resistance of the external circuit increases. At the same charging current, the amount of heat generated also increases, resulting in a decrease in the thermal runaway start time.

claims.claim: In a 12-cell Li-ion battery pack charged at 2C, increasing the number of parallel groups (while decreasing series count) decreases thermal runaway start time and increases both the number of thermal runaway batteries and maximum thermal runaway temperature: 3S4P yields 2480s start, 12 batteries, 628°C; 12S yields 2710s start, 7 batteries, 504°C.
claims.quote: the start time of thermal runaway of the battery pack decreases. The number of thermal runaway batteries increases with the increase of the number of parallel groups in the battery pack. Maximum temperature of thermal runaway has an increasing trend with the increase of the number of parallel groups.

claims.claim: In all connection configurations tested, the first batteries to experience thermal runaway were consistently located in the center (middle) of each series group within the battery pack, attributed to poorer heat dissipation conditions at the center.
claims.quote: In the internal temperature field distribution of the series battery pack, the temperature in the center of the battery pack is significantly higher than the peripheral temperature due to factors such as poor heat dissipation conditions, and hence the phenomenon that the battery with thermal runaway is often located in the middle of the separate series battery pack.

claims.claim: At a charging rate of 2C with 4-cell series packs, increasing cell spacing from 2mm to 40mm increased thermal runaway start time from 2535s to 3040s and reduced the number of thermal runaway batteries from 4 to 1.
claims.quote: No.3 battery had thermal runaway, and the other batteries had no thermal runaway. The thermal runaway temperature of No. 3 battery was 280°C, the thermal runaway start time was 3040 seconds, and the thermal runaway maximum temperature was 409°C.

claims.claim: Under 1C constant current charging, when the battery pack temperature reached 95°C, the charging current rapidly decreased, possibly because high temperature caused the separator to melt and close, blocking the charging process.
claims.quote: In the 1C constant current charging process of the battery pack, when the temperature of the battery pack reached 95°C, the charging current rapidly decreased. The reason for this phenomenon might be that the high temperature of the battery caused the separator to melt and close, blocking the charging process.

claims.claim: For cylindrical battery packs charged at 1C and 1.5C, batteries swelled but no thermal runaway occurred; thermal runaway only occurred when the charging power exceeded 2C.
claims.quote: They found that at 1C and 1.5C, the battery in the battery pack swelled but no thermal runaway occurred. When the charging power was greater than 2C, thermal runaway occurred.

claims.claim: The major heat source during thermal runaway of LiNixCoyMnzO2 cathode lithium-ion batteries is the oxidation-reduction reaction between the cathode and anode.
claims.quote: the major heat source is the oxidation-reduction reaction

claims.claim: The internal short circuit (ISC) is critical to trigger the oxidation-reduction reaction during thermal runaway, but ISC is not the major heat source that heats the cell to 800°C or more.
claims.quote: the internal short circuit (ISC) is critical to trigger the oxidation-reduction reaction; 4) the ISC is not the major heat source that heat the cell to 800℃ or more

claims.claim: The oxidation-reduction reaction between cathode and anode is triggered when the temperature reaches a critical initiation temperature; for Battery Sample A this onset temperature is 250°C, and for Battery Sample B it is 270°C.
claims.quote: The T0,x for the reaction is quite high (250℃ for Battery Sample A, and 270℃ for Battery Sample B), indicating that once the temperature of the SYSBAT reaches 250℃ or higher, and the separator already collapses, there will be fierce reaction between the cathode and the anode, releasing large amount of heat generation.

claims.claim: Fire releases large amounts of heat during thermal runaway, but most of that heat is not used to heat the cell itself.
claims.quote: the fire releases lots of heat, but most of the heat is not used to heat the cell itself

claims.claim: The collapse temperature of a PE+ceramic-coated separator can be much higher than the melting point of the PE base (approximately 130°C); with tight compression the separator can hold to as high as 260°C, but soaking with electrolyte advances shrinkage to lower temperature.
claims.quote: the pure PE based separator will collapse at 130oC, the PE based separator with ceramic coating can hold to much higher temperature, usually higher than 200℃… with tight compression inside the battery cell, the separator with PE base+ceramic coating can hold to as high as 260℃… the shrinkage will be advanced to lower temperature if the separator is soaked with electrolyte.

claims.claim: When cell temperature reaches 100–110°C, exceeding the boiling points of DMC and EMC, venting with white-grey smoke occurs; this constitutes the first observable smoke or fire event during thermal runaway tests.
claims.quote: when the temperature of the cell (TBAT) reaches 100~110℃, which exceeds the boiling point of DMC and EMC, venting with white-grey smoke will be observed during TR tests. This is the first chance for the experimentalists to observe smoke/fire in their TR tests.

claims.claim: Liu et al. (2018) suggests that internal short circuit is not an essential condition for thermal runaway, indicating that thermal runaway can occur without ISC.
claims.quote: Liu et al. (Liu et al., 2018) suggests that the ISC is not an essential condition for TR. Some hints are indicating that there can be TR without ISC.

claims.claim: The degree of ISC can change the moment of thermal runaway trigger even among batteries produced in the same batch; a milder ISC delays TR to a higher temperature, representing better safety.
claims.quote: the degree of ISC can change the moment of TR, even if the batteries are produced in a same batch. And… if the separator can mild the ISC during its breakage, the TR may be delayed to a higher temperature, which represents a better safety.

claims.claim: The mass exhausted during thermal runaway (cell venting) can vary from 10% to 70% based on experimental experience, making consideration of cell venting important in TR models when mass loss is large.
claims.quote: The mass exhausting during TR can vary from 10% to 70%, as seen from experimental experiences. Therefore considering cell venting in the TR model is important, if the mass loss is huge.

claims.claim: The third stage of smoke/fire during thermal runaway is more prone to fire and explosion because it contains a high proportion of organic gases at temperatures much higher than the first and second stages, accompanied by melting of the aluminum current collector at its melting point of 660°C.
claims.quote: a third jet smoke/fire is possible after the TR is triggered. The electrode powders will burst out with the gases generated during the oxidation-reduction reactions… because the aluminum current collector will melt into fragments at its melting point of 660℃… As the smoke in the third stage contains much proportion of organic gases, and the temperature of the gases is much higher than that in the first and the second stages, fire and explosion will be more prone to occur in this stage.

claims.claim: The maximum battery voltage tracing error for the proposed fractional impedance model (FIM) combined with the least square genetic algorithm (LSGA) is within 0.5%.
claims.quote: The maximum battery voltage tracing error for the proposed model and parameter identification method is within 0.5%; this demonstrates the good performance of the model and the efficiency of the least square genetic algorithm to estimate the internal parameters of lithium-ion batteries.

claims.claim: During UDDS testing, the voltage tracing errors based on the 2-RC and recursive least squares (RLS) method vary within a large range, exceeding 0.25 V, whereas the FIM&LSGA tracing error curve is almost a straight line close to zero and mostly within an error bound of 0.02 V.
claims.quote: The tracing error curve based on FIM&LSGA is almost a straight line, close to zero, and mostly with an error bound of 0.02 V. On the other hand, the tracing errors based on 2-RC&RLS vary within a large range, even > 0.25 V.

claims.claim: In the low-frequency EIS region (0.005 Hz–0.05 Hz), the HPPC test results indicate the low-frequency plot should be regarded as part of a depressed semicircle with a large diameter rather than a straight line, leading to use of a parallel combination instead of a Warburg element.
claims.quote: The HPPC test results indicate that the low-frequency plot should be regarded as a part of a depressed semicircle with a large diameter rather than a straight line. Thus, a parallel combination can be used to explain the depressed semicircle in the low-frequency section.

claims.claim: In the high-frequency EIS region (1 Hz–5000 Hz), the battery transient response is associated with Ohmic polarization and can be simplified as a single resistor rather than the complex high-frequency ECM.
claims.quote: The battery response in the rapidly rising stage could be associated with the EIS response in the high-frequency region. The battery voltage increased rapidly due to Ohmic polarization; this can be simplistically modeled by a resistor, instead of the complex model in the high-frequency region.

claims.claim: Electrochemical models such as pseudo-two-dimensional models and single particle models are more accurate than equivalent circuit models (ECMs) but require a large number of parameters that cannot be measured.
claims.quote: electrochemical models such as pseudo-two-dimensional models [18], single particle models, and extended single particle models [19] are more accurate than ECMs; however, they require a large number of parameters that cannot be measured.

claims.claim: A genetic algorithm-based parameter identification method applied to a LiFePO4 cell electrochemical model estimated cell voltage and power with a relative error of 5%, which was higher than expected.
claims.quote: Joel et al. [26] proposed a parameter identification method based on a genetic algorithm (GA) for a LiFePO4 cell electrochemical model. Cell voltage and power were estimated with a relative error of 5%, a value higher than expected.

claims.claim: Batteries cycled at −10 °C reached 90% SOH after only 12 cycles, whereas batteries cycled at room temperature reached 90% SOH after 250 cycles.
claims.quote: The batteries that cycled at −10 °C exhibited rapid degradation and reached 90% SOH after only 12 cycles, whereas the batteries cycled at RT exhibited a better performance, with the cycle numbers reaching 250 cycles.

claims.claim: Low temperature increases the internal resistance of the battery, reduces the battery capacity, and accelerates aging.
claims.quote: This is because a low temperature increases the internal resistance of the battery, reduces the battery capacity, and accelerates aging.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the maximum temperature during thermal runaway propagation exceeded 600 °C, with one cell (Bat2) reaching 733 °C.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature that can be reached with 100% SOC is above 600 °C, with Bat2 even reaching 733 °C.

claims.claim: At 50% SOC, the maximum temperature during thermal runaway propagation reached approximately 500 °C, whereas at 0% SOC the maximum temperature was only 330 °C.
claims.quote: The battery module temperature at 50% SOC was lower than that at 100% SOC, reaching about 500 °C, whereas the maximum temperature at 0% SOC was only 330 °C.

claims.claim: At 0% SOC, only Bat1 experienced thermal runaway, while Bat2 and Bat3 exhibited only internal short-circuits without a violent reaction, because reduced lithium ions in the anode and negligible oxygen released by the cathode cannot react further with the electrolyte and anode during TR.
claims.quote: The TR of the battery module with 0% SOC is not evident. Only Bat1 experienced TR, whereas Bat2 and Bat3 only exhibited internal short-circuits without a violent reaction. This is because as SOC decreases, the reduction of lithium ions in the anode and the negligible amount of oxygen released by the cathode material cannot react further with the electrolyte and anode during TR.

claims.claim: The increase in temperature rate of the battery module at 100% SOC exceeded 90 °C/s, whereas at 50% SOC it reached nearly 30 °C/s, and only Bat1 at 0% SOC exceeded the 1 °C/s thermal runaway threshold.
claims.quote: The increase in temperature rate of the battery module at 100% SOC exceeded 90 °C/s, whereas at 50% SOC, it was lower, but still reached nearly 30 °C/s. Only the increase in temperature rate of Bat1 at 0% SOC exceeded 1 °C/s.

claims.claim: A higher SOC results in more oxygen being released due to electrode decomposition, which reacts with the electrolyte to generate additional heat, causing faster and more intense heat release.
claims.quote: This is because a higher SOC results in more oxygen being released due to electrode decomposition. The released oxygen then reacted with the electrolyte, generating additional heat. As a result, the heat release became faster and more intense, leading to a more pronounced and violent thermal response.

claims.claim: The total mass loss during thermal runaway propagation for fresh battery modules was 45 g at 100% SOC, 25.3 g at 50% SOC, and 3.4 g at 0% SOC.
claims.quote: The module at 0% SOC experienced a total mass loss of 3.4 g. At 50% SOC, the curve exhibits a total mass loss of 25.3 g... The 100% SOC battery module experienced a total mass loss of 45 g.

claims.claim: Maximum CO emissions during thermal runaway of fresh battery modules with 100% SOH were 3229 ppm at 100% SOC, 597 ppm at 50% SOC, and 96 ppm at 0% SOC.
claims.quote: The maximum CO emissions for 100% SOC, 50% SOC, and 0% SOC can reach 3229 ppm, 597 ppm, and 96 ppm, respectively.

claims.claim: Compared with fresh battery modules, room-temperature cyclic aged battery modules (90% SOH) experienced greater total mass loss during thermal runaway propagation at 50% SOC (35.5 g vs. 25.3 g).
claims.quote: Compared with the fresh modules, the aging battery module experienced greater mass loss... 100%SOH,50%SOC... Total loss:25.3 g... RT 90%SOH,50%SOC... Total loss:35.5 g

claims.claim: Cyclic aging advances the thermal runaway onset time of battery modules compared with fresh battery modules, due to SEI film instability caused by repeated rupture and reformation during charge-discharge cycles.
claims.quote: Comparing the fresh battery modules, the aging battery module's TR start time is advanced... the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film on the negative electrode's surface ruptures. Once the SEI film is ruptured, the negative electrode reacts with the electrolyte to form a new SEI film, and this process is repeated, which causes SEI instability and poor thermal stability.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC, the maximum temperature rate after room-temperature cyclic aging (90% SOH) was lower than for fresh modules (e.g., Bat1: 116.5 °C/s vs. 126.8 °C/s), attributed to reduced internal capacity and consumption of electrode materials and lithium ions during aging.
claims.quote: For 100% SOC, the increase in temperature rate after aging is lower than that of the non-aging battery module. This can be attributed to the aging battery's reduced internal capacity, as well as the consumption of positive and negative electrode materials and lithium ions during the cyclic aging process.

claims.claim: For 50% and 0% SOC, the maximum temperature rate after room-temperature cyclic aging was larger than that of non-aged battery modules, because the aging battery accumulated heat during early stages and then experienced a violent burst during TR.
claims.quote: However, for 50% and 0% SOC, the increase in temperature rate was larger than that of the non-aging battery modules, because the aging battery accumulated heat during the early stages, then experienced a violent burst during TR.

claims.claim: The total TRP duration for battery modules aged at −10 °C was shorter than for modules aged at room temperature: 197 s vs. 228 s at 100% SOC, and 284 s vs. 290 s at 50% SOC.
claims.quote: The total times for the TRP of the battery module at 100% SOC and 50% SOC are 197 s and 284 s, respectively. These times are lower than those observed under RT aging, which are 228 s for 100% SOC and 290 s for 50% SOC.

claims.claim: After low-temperature aging (−10 °C), lithium ions in the anode form lithium dendrites that are more prone to reacting with the electrolyte than graphite, resulting in poor thermal stability and a higher likelihood of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: This is because after low-temperature aging, the numerous lithium ions in the anode form lithium dendrites that are more prone to reacting with the electrolyte than graphite, resulting in poor thermal stability and a higher likelihood of TR.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the total mass loss of battery modules aged at −10 °C (54.2 g) was greater than that of modules aged at room temperature (45.1 g), attributed to SEI layer decomposition and structural changes from reduced lithium content in the cathode.
claims.quote: At 100% SOC, the mass loss of individual cells after low-temperature aging was higher than that after RT aging... Total mass loss:54.2 g... Total mass loss:45.1 g... this phenomenon can be attributed to the high surface area of the lithium anode, which promotes the formation of a number of SEI layers on the surface of the lithium metal. These SEI layers subsequently undergo decomposition.

claims.claim: At 50% SOC, the mass loss during TR was lower for modules aged at −10 °C (20.5 g) than for modules aged at room temperature (35.5 g), because early safety valve rupture in the low-temperature aged modules led to minor internal pressure loss and inability to eject excess gas.
claims.quote: At 50% SOC, the mass loss of TR after RT and low-temperature aging was 35.5 g and 20.5 g, respectively. This was because of an early rupture in the safety valve of the low-temperature aging battery module, which led to a minor internal pressure loss of the battery and an inability to eject excess gas.

claims.claim: The heat transfer between adjacent cells during thermal runaway propagation was less than 25 kJ across all tested conditions.
claims.quote: The heat transfer between adjacent batteries was found to be less than 25 kJ.

claims.claim: Battery modules with higher SOC require less heat transfer from adjacent cells to trigger thermal runaway propagation, at the same SOH.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway propagation between cells with higher SOCs requires less heat at the same SOH.

claims.claim: Average heat transfer between adjacent cells increases with the degree of aging, with fresh 100% SOC modules averaging 11828 J, RT-aged 90% SOH 100% SOC modules averaging 13934 J, and −10 °C-aged 90% SOH 100% SOC modules averaging 15159 J.
claims.quote: aging increases the average heat transfer between the same SOC battery modules... 100% SOH,100% SOC: 11828 J... RT,90% SOH, 100% SOC: 13934 J... −10 °C, 90% SOH, 100% SOC: 15159 J

claims.claim: In an open environment, flame radiation contributes only a little to the overall heat transfer between batteries; the primary mode of heat transfer involves contact between battery shells.
claims.quote: Studies have demonstrated that in an open environment, flame radiation contributes only a little to the overall heat transfer. The primary mode of heat transfer for the battery involves contact between battery shells.

claims.claim: LiPF6 begins to decompose into LiF and PF5 at approximately 107°C.
claims.quote: LiPF6 begins to decompose into LiF and PF5 at approximately 107 ◦C.

claims.claim: PF5 and POF3, the thermal decomposition products of LiPF6, can catalyze the chain oxidation reactions of organic solvents, reducing the critical temperature for thermal runaway by 10–30°C.
claims.quote: PF5 and POF3, the thermal decomposition products of LiPF6, can catalyze the chain oxidation reactions of organic solvents, reducing the critical temperature for thermal runaway by 10–30 ◦C.

claims.claim: The minimum energy required for ring-opening reactions of EC alone is 290.89 kJ/mol, but after combining with PF5, the ring-opening energy of EC decreases to 185.37 kJ/mol, representing a reduction of 36.27%.
claims.quote: The minimum energy required for the ring-opening reactions of EC is 290.89 kJ/mol [21,32], while after combining with PF5, the ring-opening energy of EC decreases to 185.37 kJ/mol, representing a reduction of 36.27%.

claims.claim: POF3 exhibits a weaker catalytic effect on promoting the thermal decomposition reactions of EC than PF5, because the heat absorbed during ring-opening reactions after POF3 combines with EC is higher than after PF5 combines with EC.
claims.quote: Due to the fact that the oxidizability of POF3 is weaker than that of PF5, the heat absorbed during the ring-opening reactions after POF3 combines with EC is higher than that after PF5 combines with EC, whether in the electrolyte environment or the air environment. Thus, POF3 exhibits a weaker catalytic effect on promoting the thermal decomposition reactions of EC than that of PF5.

claims.claim: In the electrolyte environment, the energy consumed by the combination of FDMC with PF5 and POF3 increases by 733.67% and 36.33% respectively compared with DMC, and in the air environment it rises by 105.73% and 74.46% respectively.
claims.quote: Compared with DMC, in the electrolyte environment, the energy consumed by the combination of FDMC with PF5 and POF3 increases by 733.67% and 36.33%, respectively; in the air environment, the energy consumed by the combination of FDMC with PF5 and POF3 rises by 105.73% and 74.46%, respectively.

claims.claim: In the electrolyte environment, the energy consumed in the thermal decomposition reactions of FDEC after binding with PF5 or POF3 increases by 212.40 kJ/mol and 208.71 kJ/mol respectively compared with DEC, representing rises of 571.74% and 631.22%.
claims.quote: Compared with DEC, the energy consumed during the binding process of FDEC with PF5 or POF3 decreases slightly, but the energy consumed in the subsequent thermal decomposition reactions increases by 212.40 kJ/mol and 208.71 kJ/mol, respectively (rising by 571.74% and 631.22%, respectively).

claims.claim: DEC is more prone to combining with PF5 and POF3 and undergoing thermal decomposition reactions than DMC, because the bond energy of the C-C bond in the C2H5- group of DEC (approximately 347 kJ/mol) is lower than that of the C-H bond in the CH3- group of DMC (approximately 413 kJ/mol).
claims.quote: DEC is more likely to combine with PF5 and undergo the first step of thermal decomposition to generate fluorinated hydrocarbons and chain phosphate groups. This is because the bond energy of the C-C bond in the C2H5 − group of DEC (approximately 347 kJ/mol) is lower than that of the C-H bond in the CH3 − group of DMC (approximately 413 kJ/mol).

claims.claim: When battery thermal runaway is triggered by puncture or short circuit, the battery temperature rises rapidly with a heating rate of up to 100°C/min, and the maximum temperature may exceed 600°C.
claims.quote: For thermal runaway caused by puncture or short circuit, the battery temperature rises rapidly, with a heating rate of up to 100 ◦C/min, and the maximum temperature may exceed 600 ◦C.

claims.claim: As the temperature rises to 120–200°C during LIB thermal runaway, lithium intercalated in the negative electrode reacts with organic solvents in the electrolyte, generating Li2CO3 and flammable gases including CO2, CO, and H2 and releasing a large amount of heat.
claims.quote: As the temperature rises to 120–200 ◦C, the lithium intercalated in the negative electrode reacts with organic solvents in the electrolyte (such as ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), and diethyl carbonate (DEC)), generating Li2CO3 and flammable gases like, CO2, CO, and H2 and releasing a large amount of heat.

claims.claim: When the temperature increases to 200–300°C during LIB thermal runaway, positive electrode materials decompose, releasing O2 which reacts with organic solvents in the electrolyte and releasing a large amount of heat.
claims.quote: When the temperature further increases to 200–300 ◦C, positive electrode materials (such as Li2CoO2, LiFePO4, and LiNixCoyMnzO3) decompose, releasing O2 which reacts with organic solvents in the electrolyte and releasing a large amount of heat.

claims.claim: The P atoms in PF5 or POF3 combine with the O atoms of the ether oxygen groups in carbonates, while the F atoms combine with the C atoms adjacent to the ether oxygen groups, promoting ring-opening or chain scission of carbonate molecules.
claims.quote: The P atoms in PF5 or POF3 combine with the O atoms of the ether oxygen groups in carbonates, while the F atoms combine with the C atoms adjacent to the ether oxygen groups. This promotes the ring-opening or chain scission of carbonate molecules, reduces the energy required for the reaction, and accelerates the thermal decomposition reaction and the generation of fire gases.

claims.claim: Replacing LiPF6 with more stable lithium salts such as LiFSI, LiBF, or LiTFSI can eliminate the formation of PF5 and POF3 at the source, because these alternative lithium salts do not contain labile P-F bonds in their molecular structures.
claims.quote: If LiPF6 is replaced with more stable lithium salts such as LiFSI, LiBF, or LiTFSI, the formation of PF5 and POF3 can be eliminated at the source. Such alternative lithium salts do not contain labile P-F bonds in their molecular structures, so they are less prone to decomposing and producing reactive catalytic substances under high-temperature conditions.

claims.claim: SEI metastable component (CH2OCO2Li)2 depletion begins below 100°C with maximum values around 110°C, as verified by Richard and Dahn.
claims.quote: The start of this depletion was verified in temperature values bellow 100 oC, with its maximum values around 110 oC according to Richard and Dahn [28].

claims.claim: The energy release and shape of the heat release peak during SEI decomposition are strongly influenced by electrolyte composition.
claims.quote: Spotnitz and Franklin [31] report that the energy release and the shape of the peak in the process are strongly influenced by the electrolyte composition.

claims.claim: Electrolyte reacts with LiPF6 salt in temperature ranges from 200°C to 230°C, as found by Wang et al. and Campion et al., but this was not observed by Yang et al.
claims.quote: Authors as Wang et al. [32] and Campion et al. [33] have found evidence that the electrolyte reacts with LiPF6 salt in temperature ranges from 200 oC to 230 oC. Nonetheless, these evidences were not observed by Yang et al. [34].

claims.claim: Once the SEI decomposes, ethylene carbonate (EC) can reach the graphite surface and react with intercalated Li+ in highly exothermic reactions that form an inorganic layer, slowing down the process.
claims.quote: once the SEI decomposes, ethylene carbonate (EC) may reach the surface of the graphite. Therefore, it can start to react with the intercalated Li+ by means of different reactions... both reactions are highly exothermic, resulting in a significant temperature increase. Nonetheless, they originate an inorganic layer that leads to a slowdown of the process.

claims.claim: The new SEI formed by EC-Li reactions prevents further EC-Li reactions for temperatures below 250°C, but above this threshold the new SEI is also consumed, contributing to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Kriston et al. [35] also suggest that the new SEI prevent further EC-Li reactions for temperatures below 250 oC. Nonetheless, as the temperature rises above this threshold, the new SEI is also consumed, contributing to the thermal runaway process.

claims.claim: LiCoO2 cathode decomposition reactions occur from 220°C to 500°C, as reported by Biensan et al.
claims.quote: Biensan et al. [39] reported that the decomposition reactions occur from 220 oC to 500 oC.

claims.claim: NMC cathode has one of the highest thermal stability among evaluated cathode materials together with LiMn2O4, which requires temperatures higher than 275°C to be decomposed.
claims.quote: NMC cathode has one of the highest thermal stability among those evaluated together with the LiMn2O4, which requires temperatures higher than 275 oC to be decomposed.

claims.claim: High oxygen generation from NMC cathode occurs at temperatures higher than 200°C, as observed by Ren et al.
claims.quote: Ren et al. [43] have also monitored the oxygen evolution of the cathode, observing a high oxygen generation from temperatures higher than 200 oC.

claims.claim: The heat released by cathode decomposition is significantly lower than that from anode reactions.
claims.quote: It is also worth to mention that the heat released by the cathode decomposition was significantly lower than that from the anode reactions.

claims.claim: Binder decomposition in the NMC cathode requires temperatures in the range from 400°C to 500°C to initiate.
claims.quote: concluding that temperatures in the range from 400 oC to 500 oC are required to initiate this reaction.

claims.claim: Binder decomposition on the anode side occurs at much lower temperatures, from 270°C, as concluded by Kriston et al. using simultaneous heat flow and FTIR analysis.
claims.quote: the simultaneous analysis of heat flow and Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) have allowed kriston et al. to conclude that the binder decomposition occurs at much lower temperatures, from 270 oC.

claims.claim: With the Kriston mechanism, at oven temperatures around 130°C, partial SEI decomposition occurs but the battery does not enter thermal runaway because the energy released is absorbed by the oven.
claims.quote: at low oven temperatures (≈130 oC), in spite of having a partial decomposition of the SEI (see Figure 10), the battery does not enter in a thermal runaway process. This can be attributed to the fact that the energy released in the process can be absorbed by the oven.

claims.claim: With the Kriston mechanism, the critical oven temperature to trigger thermal runaway is 160°C, at which point cathode decomposition is enabled when the battery temperature reaches 200°C.
claims.quote: Once the oven achieves the critical temperature value of 160 oC, the components decomposition leads to a significant temperature increase, achieving the temperature threshold of 200 oC, which enables the cathode decomposition (the most exothermic process).

claims.claim: The Ren et al. mechanism predicts thermal runaway onset at 136°C oven temperature, which is 24°C lower than the 160°C required by the Kriston mechanism.
claims.quote: While the mechanism from Kriston et al. only reaches this condition with oven temperatures of 160 oC, the mechanism from Ren et al. already fulfill conditions for BTR at 136 oC.

claims.claim: The Feng et al. mechanism requires oven temperatures higher than 200°C to trigger thermal runaway, which is the highest onset temperature among the three NMC111 mechanisms evaluated.
claims.quote: this mechanism presents the highest temperature onset for thermal runaway prediction, requiring oven temperatures higher than 200 oC to observe the phenomena.

claims.claim: The Feng et al. mechanism uses a temperature threshold to activate cathode decomposition, resulting in no noticeable difference in battery temperature as oven temperature increases from 175°C to 210°C, which is characterized as a non-physical behavior.
claims.quote: the mechanism considers a temperature threshold to activate the cathode decomposition reaction. In this sense, despite of the temperature increases from 175 oC to 210 oC, no noticeable difference is perceived in the battery temperature. This feature leads to a non-physical relation between the issue and the modelling approach.

claims.claim: The maximum battery temperature achieved after SEI, cathode, and anode are consumed is approximately 700°C for the Kriston mechanism.
claims.quote: the maximum battery temperature achieved once the SEI, cathode and anode are consumed is very similar, with maximum values of almost 700 oC.

claims.claim: Despite small differences in DSC results among NMC111 mechanisms, ARC simulations show significant differences in thermal runaway prediction.
claims.quote: It was demonstrated that despite of small differences in the DSC, the results from ARC presents significant differences in the thermal runaway prediction.

claims.claim: The Kriston mechanism uses 9 different reactions to describe the thermal runaway process, while the Kim mechanism uses only 4 reactions.
claims.quote: the mechanism proposed by Kim relies on using 4 reactions in total to describe the thermal runaway process. By contrast, Kriston´s mechanism uses 9 different reactions, providing a more comprehensive relation between the numerical model and the phenomena in discussion.

claims.claim: The Kim mechanism predicts cathode reactions initiating almost 50°C earlier than the Bilyaz mechanism.
claims.quote: Kim et al. mechanism predicts that the cathode reactions initiate almost 50 oC earlier than the mechanism proposed by Byliaz et al.

claims.claim: The Kim mechanism's earlier and more localized heat release distribution is suggested to lead to an early occurrence of thermal runaway compared to mechanisms with more distributed reaction rates.
claims.quote: Kim mechanism has a much earlier peak of heat release which cannot be correlated with any of the reactions observed in Kriston mechanism. Therefore, it is suggested that this mechanism may lead to an early occurrence of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The Kriston mechanism provides a less binary (more continuous) transition between thermal runaway and non-thermal runaway conditions compared to Ren and Feng mechanisms, which exhibit almost binary behavior.
claims.quote: Both mechanisms proposed by Ren et al. and Feng et al. have provided an almost binary behavior between conditions that have TR and conditions where TR is not present. The mechanism suggested by Kriston et al. seems to have more continuity between the reactions.

claims.claim: The Feng mechanism validation could not be accomplished due to the absence of important parameters needed to replicate the accelerated rate calorimeter test used in its original investigation.
claims.quote: Feng mechanism validation was not accomplished due to different reasons. The main limiting factor was the absence of important parameters to replicate the accelerate rate calorimeter test that was used in its investigation.

claims.claim: The Kim-Hatchard mechanism required modification of the surface-to-volume ratio from 48.37 to 300 to match the experimental heating rate of the battery.
claims.quote: The best results were obtained by modifying the surface-to-volume ratio from the original value of 48.37 to 300. With this adjustment, the heating rate of the battery was perfectly matched at the same time of the consumption of the components.

claims.claim: The Ren et al. mechanism lacks a formulation for electrolyte evaporation, and the current study proposes including electrolyte evaporation equations from the Kriston mechanism to account for this effect, based on an observed sharp decrease in heating rate around 150°C.
claims.quote: In spite of mentioning that this phenomenon has a fundamental role in the early stages of the thermal runaway reactions, no specific formulation was proposed... a sharp decrease in the heating rate can be observed around 150 0C, which could be associated to the evaporation of the electrolyte. As a solution for these issues, the inclusion of the electrolyte evaporation equations from the mechanism proposed by Kriston et al. to consider the impact of this component in the problem description is proposed.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway can be triggered by mechanical, electrical, and thermal abuse conditions, and once initiated, it produces flammable gases and oxygen that can sustain flame outside the cell and propagate thermal runaway to neighboring cells.
claims.quote: This phenomenon can be originated by several type of abuse conditions as mechanical, electrical and thermal abuse [18]... This process also forms flammable gases and oxygen, which can be ignited by different mechanisms as high local temperatures, short-circuit, etc. [20]. This flame can be sustained out of the cell while the gases are vented and provoke the thermal runaway of the neighbourhood cells.

claims.claim: For 25 types of prismatic lithium-ion cells with capacities between 8 and 145 Ah, mass loss during thermal runaway is on average 7.05 g Ah−1 with a coefficient of determination R2 = 0.88, following an approximately linear dependency on cell capacity.
claims.quote: the tested cells lost on average 7.05 g Ah−1 of their mass during TR (R2 = 0.88).

claims.claim: Total mass loss during thermal runaway is distributed into 5.00 g Ah−1 particles and liquid components (R2 = 0.80) and 2.05 g Ah−1 venting gas (R2 = 0.94), corresponding to 70.9% particles and 29.1% gas by mass.
claims.quote: This mass loss is distributed into 5.00 g Ah−1 particles and liquid components (R2 = 0.80) and 2.05 g Ah−1 venting gas (R2 = 0.94). Consequently, the gas makes up 29.1% gas and particles and liquid components make up 70.9% of the total mass loss.

claims.claim: Vented gas mass during thermal runaway shows smaller variance (1.6–2.7 g Ah−1) compared to vented particle mass (2.9–7.2 g Ah−1), indicating that generated gas is mainly dependent on cell capacity and cannot be actively influenced by cell design, whereas vented particle mass is individual for each cell type.
claims.quote: The vented gas is subject to a smaller variance (1.6–2.7 g Ah−1) than the vented particles (2.9–7.2 g Ah−1). Therefore, it is concluded that the amount of vented gas is mainly dependent on the cell capacity and cannot be actively influenced by e.g. the cell design. In contrast, the vented particle mass is individual for each cell (type) and depends on several parameters.

claims.claim: Prismatic lithium-ion cells with high gravimetric energy density tend to show a higher fraction of vented gas and a lower fraction of vented particles in their total mass loss during thermal runaway compared to cells with low gravimetric energy density.
claims.quote: Cells with a high grav. energy density tend to show a higher fraction of gas in their mass loss compared to cells with a low gravimetric energy density.

claims.claim: Total generated heat during thermal runaway is on average 19.45 kJ Ah−1 (R2 = 0.96), which is on average 1.41 times higher than the electrical energy stored in the cell, with a maximum ratio of 1.72.
claims.quote: Qtot is on average 19.45 kJ Ah−1 (R2 = 0.96)... the ratio between total generated heat and electrical energy stored in the cell was found to be on average 1.41. The maximum value was 1.72.

claims.claim: The distribution of total generated heat between heat remaining in the cell (Q1) and heat transported by venting products (Q2) depends on the mass loss during thermal runaway, such that increasing mass remaining in the cell leads to higher Q1.
claims.quote: The distribution of the total generated heat into the fraction of heat remaining in the cell (Q1) and the fraction of heat that is transported by the venting products (Q2) depends on the mass loss during TR... with increasing mass remaining in the cell mcell,TR more heat remains in the cell as well and therefore Q1 is higher.

claims.claim: Nail-penetrated cells show a lower mass loss during thermal runaway compared to overheated (thermally triggered) cells, with one explanation being that the nail lodged inside the cell prevents particle emission and inner cell parts from moving.
claims.quote: In previous publications it is stated that nail penetrated cells show a lower mass loss in comparison to overheated cells. Essl et al. explained this behavior with the 'nail inside the cell [that] may have prevented further particle emission.'

claims.claim: During nail-penetration thermal runaway tests there is no minor pre-runaway venting sequence, unlike thermal triggering which produces one minor venting before the TR and one major venting when TR occurs, and therefore the vented gas amount may be lower in nail-penetration tests.
claims.quote: With thermal triggering of the TR there are two sequences of gas venting: one minor venting before the TR due to the rising temperature inside the cell and one major venting when the TR occurs. During nail-penetration tests there is no minor venting and therefore the vented gas amount may be lower.

claims.claim: The vented gas composition from a 53 Ah NMC111 prismatic cell triggered by nail penetration in inert atmosphere consisted mainly of H2 (32.66 vol%), CO (31.34 vol%), and CO2 (27.34 vol%), with additional C2H4 (4.32 vol%) and CH4 (4.33 vol%), yielding an average molar mass of 23.38 g mol−1.
claims.quote: The main detected components were H2, CO and CO2 with a concentration of cH2 = 32.66 Vol.-%, cCO = 31.34 Vol.-% and cCO2 = 27.34 Vol.-%, respectively. Additional detected components were C2H4 with a concentration of c = 4.32 Vol.-% and CH4 with a concentration of cCH4 = 4.33 Vol.-%. ...Molar mass Mgas... 23.38 g mol−1.

claims.claim: The tested prismatic lithium-ion cells generated on average 2.14 L Ah−1 of venting gas during thermal runaway at SATP conditions, with a range of 1.6–2.8 L Ah−1, which is higher than the 1.96 L Ah−1 reported by Koch et al. for cells triggered by over-temperature.
claims.quote: the data of this study results in a mean generated gas volume of 2.14 ℓ Ah−1. This is higher than the results of Koch et al. for 41 pouch and 10 prismatic cells triggered by over-temperature (1.96 ℓ Ah−1).

claims.claim: In a lithium-ion battery, ohmic resistance consists of electrode materials resistance, electrolytes resistance, separators resistance, and contact resistance among various components.
claims.quote: The ohmic resistance consists of electrode materials resistance, electrolytes resistance, separators resistance and contact resistance among various components.

claims.claim: Increase of battery internal resistance lowers battery discharge voltage and shortens discharge time.
claims.quote: Increase of battery internal resistance would lower battery discharge voltage, shorten discharge time, and have a direct impact on the battery's power characteristics.

claims.claim: In accelerated life testing of an 8Ah lithium manganate battery at 40°C with 4C full charge and discharge, the increase in polarization internal resistance is much smaller than the increase in ohmic internal resistance, so the major part of battery internal resistance increase is caused by ohmic internal resistance.
claims.quote: increased range of polarization internal resistance obvious much smaller than ohmic internal resistance, therefore the major part of battery internal resistance increase is caused by ohm internal resistance.

claims.claim: Battery ohmic internal resistance increases faster as charging and discharging current increases, under constant temperature of 40°C.
claims.quote: the battery ohmic internal resistance increases faster along with the enhancement of charging and discharging current.

claims.claim: Battery ohmic internal resistance increases as depth of discharge (DOD) increases, under constant temperature and constant current conditions.
claims.quote: The battery ohmic internal resistance increases along with the enhancement of DOD.

claims.claim: Among the three major factors affecting lithium-ion battery ohmic internal resistance (ambient temperature, current, and DOD), ambient temperature is the most influential, while current and DOD have relatively weaker influence.
claims.quote: Discussing the three major factors effecting lithium-ion battery interface resistance, ambient temperature is the most influential one; the influence of current and DOD are relatively weak.

claims.claim: In the lithium-ion battery voltage platform area where SOC is between 30% and 80%, ohmic internal resistance basically remains stable and is largely unaffected by SOC or different current excitations.
claims.quote: in lithium-ion battery voltage platform area (SOC is between 30% and 80%), ohmic internal resistance of battery basically maintains stable, battery's SOC condition has very small influence on it; and under the different current excitation, the ohmic internal resistance of lithium-ion battery almost has no change.

claims.claim: Using the recursive least squares identification algorithm on the simplified equivalent circuit model, ohmic internal resistance converges rapidly within 15 seconds from an initial value of 1 mΩ, with precision satisfying actual requirements.
claims.quote: freely picking the initial value of resistance(using 1mΩ as initial value of resistance in Figure 8), the ohmic internal resistance will converge rapidly in 15 seconds, the convergence curves is close; its precision satisfies the actual request basically.

claims.claim: Comparing ohmic internal resistance obtained by parameter identification of the simplified equivalent model with actual values from impulse discharge method, the error can be controlled within 2%.
claims.quote: Comparing the ohmic internal resistance obtained by parameter identification of this equivalent model with the actual value of resistance obtained by impulsive discharge method, the error can be controlled in 2%.

claims.claim: The maximum SOC estimation error of the proposed DD-EKF method under DST conditions is 0.223%, while the ampere-hour integration method has a maximum error of 1.112% under the same conditions.
claims.quote: the SOC prediction value of the proposed DD-EKF method converges to the actual value in a short time with a maximum error of 0.223%, while the SOC predicted through the ampere-hour integration method has a maximum error of 1.112%

claims.claim: The maximum SOC prediction errors of the proposed DD-EKF method are 4.57% under BBDST conditions and 0.223% under DST conditions.
claims.quote: The maximum SOC prediction errors of the proposed method are 4.57% and 0.223% in the BBDST and DST conditions, respectively

claims.claim: Under high process and observation noise, the maximum SOC prediction error of the proposed DD-EKF method is 3.87%.
claims.quote: the maximum estimation error of SOC is only 3.87% under the influence of high process and observation noise

claims.claim: The battery ohmic internal resistance R0 has the highest sensitivity among all model parameters in the MF-ETC model across the entire SOC range, and its sensitivity increases slowly as SOC increases.
claims.quote: the sensitivity of the battery ohmic internal resistance R0 is the highest and increases slowly as the SOC increases

claims.claim: The greatest variations in parameter sensitivity occur in the SOC range below 20%, caused by the inability of the equivalent model to accurately characterize the battery state at low SOC.
claims.quote: The greatest variations in parameter sensitivity occur in the range of SOC 20%, and are caused by the inability of the equivalent model to accurately characterize the battery state at low SOC

claims.claim: The open-circuit voltage method for SOC estimation requires the battery to remain stationary for a long time to obtain the OCV-SOC curve, making it unsuitable for online measurement.
claims.quote: The open-circuit voltage method requires the battery to remain stationary for a long time to obtain the OCV-SOC curve, which is not suitable for online measurement

claims.claim: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy for SOC prediction requires the battery to be in a stationary state during measurement, making it difficult to implement on site.
claims.quote: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy uses battery impedance for SOC prediction. This requires the battery to be in a stationary state during the measurement process, making it difficult to implement on site

claims.claim: The proposed method has only been validated with experimental tests and needs further exploration for real-world vehicle working conditions, and the effect of a wide temperature range on battery condition estimation also needs to be investigated.
claims.quote: the method proposed in this paper has only been validated with experimental tests so needs to be further explored for real-world vehicle working conditions, while the effect of a wide temperature range on battery condition estimation also needs to be investigated

claims.claim: Identifying all model parameters over the same time scale increases the amount of calculation and aggravates the difficulty of generation of the ill-conditioned matrix as structural complexity increases.
claims.quote: as the structural complexity of the model increases, the identification of all parameters over the same time scale increases the amount of calculation and aggravates the difficulty of generation of the ill-conditioned matrix

claims.claim: In the proposed MF-ETC model, the diffusion resistance and diffusion capacitance in the concentration polarization impedance mainly represent the diffusion effect of lithium ions through the SEI film and interlayer solid particles.
claims.quote: The diffusion resistance and diffusion capacitance in the concentration polarization impedance mainly show the diffusion effect of lithium ions through the SEI film and interlayer solid particles

claims.claim: For a 314 Ah LiFePO4 battery at 100% SOC, using expansion force as a threshold provides more effective early warning when heating power is less than or equal to 800 W, while monitoring temperature threshold variations allows more timely thermal runaway warning when heating power exceeds 800 W.
claims.quote: when heating power is less than 800 W, the method of using the expansion force parameter as the threshold can be more effective for early warning; while when heating at high power (> 800 W), the change of the temperature threshold can be detected to make a more effectively warning of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: At 800 W heating power, no distinct voltage characteristic point was identified as a preventing threshold before venting for a 314 Ah LiFePO4 battery at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: However, no significant characteristic point was found for the 800 W heating power condition as a characteristic threshold before venting.

claims.claim: The time interval between the temperature characteristic threshold and thermal runaway decreases from approximately 2300 s to 1300 s as heating power increases from 400 W to 1000 W for a 314 Ah battery at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the time interval between the occurrence of thermal runaway is significantly shortened with the increase of power, which is reduced from approximately 2300–1300 s, which also indicates the difficulty of early warning under high power heating.

claims.claim: The force-temperature coupling threshold remained constant at 490 N/°C across all four heating powers (400, 600, 800, and 1000 W) for a 314 Ah battery at 100% SOC, while the time interval between this threshold and thermal runaway significantly decreased with increasing power.
claims.quote: it was found that the threshold of the ratio did not change with the increase of heating power and remained at 490 N/°C. However, the time interval between venting and thermal runaway corresponding to the feature threshold is significantly shortened with increasing power

claims.claim: At 400 W heating power, the temperature-expansion force coupling warning threshold provides a 196% advancement over single temperature or expansion force threshold warning times.
claims.quote: at a heating power of 400 W condition, the warning threshold for temperature and expansion force coupling can be advanced by 196% and 122%, respectively, compared to the warning time for temperature or expansion force threshold.

claims.claim: For a 314 Ah LiFePO4 battery heated at 500 W, no significant temperature characteristic threshold was observed at 25%, 50%, or 75% SOC, while at 100% SOC the temperature characteristic point appeared 1402 s before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: No significant features were observed at 25% to 75% SOC conditions. At 100% SOC, the temperature characteristic point is 574 s away from the vent opening and 1402 s ahead of the thermal runaway

claims.claim: With increasing SOC from 25% to 100% at 500 W heating power, the peak temperature reached during thermal runaway increases from approximately 230°C to 420°C, and the time to complete internal short circuit decreases from approximately 3200 s to 2600 s.
claims.quote: the temperature reached the peak is gradually increased from approximately 230°C–420°C, and the time for the occurrence of a complete short-circuit within the battery is also gradually reduced from approximately 3200–2600 s

claims.claim: The voltage-temperature coupling detection method is not suitable for batteries in low SOC states because the time interval from the coupling threshold to vent opening is only 218 s at 25% SOC, which is insufficient for early warning.
claims.quote: the time interval from valve opening is too short at 25% SOC by only 218 s, which is not sufficient to provide early warning. Therefore, it is speculated that the temperature voltage coupling detection method is not suitable for batteries in low SOC states.

claims.claim: The temperature-force coupling method for thermal runaway warning is more applicable to batteries at low SOC conditions, where the threshold of the ratio is more pronounced and the time interval between vent opening and thermal runaway is longer.
claims.quote: Given that the threshold of ratio is more pronounced at 25% and 50% SOC states, and the time interval between vent opening and thermal runaway is longer, it provides a more ample time for thermal runaway warning. Therefore, the method of coupling temperature and expansion force for thermal runaway warning is more suitable in low SOC conditions.

claims.claim: Voltage changes can detect early thermal runaway signs earlier than temperature or force parameters, but exhibit intermittency and have a narrow warning threshold.
claims.quote: Although voltage changes can detect early thermal runaway signs earlier than the other two parameters, they exhibit intermittency and have a narrow warning threshold.

claims.claim: After complete internal short circuit in a 314 Ah LiFePO4 battery, the voltage drops to one-third of the initial voltage before rapidly dropping to 0 V, and this stage III process lasts a short period and is irreversible.
claims.quote: the voltage drops to 1/3 of the initial voltage and then drops rapidly to 0 V. The stage Ⅲ lasts for a short period of time and is irreversible, and the battery cannot continue to work once it is completely short-circuited and it is very dangerous.

claims.claim: In the force-temperature coupling analysis, the expansion force of a 314 Ah battery rapidly rises to approximately 8000 N when the temperature reaches approximately 85°C, then sharply declines after venting with the temperature stabilizing at approximately 120°C under all four heating powers.
claims.quote: the expansion force initially increases and then quickly rises to about 8000 N as the temperature is about 85°C. After venting, the temperature stabilizes at about 120°C under all four heating powers.

claims.claim: For the 314 Ah LiFePO4 battery, the expansion force thresholds at 400 and 600 W heating power are 19.6 N/5 s, providing early warning times of 3115 s and 2670 s before thermal runaway respectively, which are longer than the corresponding temperature threshold warning times of 2337 s and 2352 s.
claims.quote: the corresponding expansion force thresholds at heating powers of 400, 600, and 800 W achieve early warning times of 3115, 2670, and 1424 s. However, the warning times by temperature threshold are advanced by 2337, 2352, and 1324 s, which are later than the force warning times.

claims.claim: Under low SOC conditions (25%–75%), the heat generated by SEI film decomposition decreases, and the exothermic process changes very uniformly, making it difficult to obtain a thermal runaway threshold from the rate of temperature change analysis.
claims.quote: Due to the reduced amount of lithium ion insertion between the negative graphite layers with lower internal charging capacity, the heat generated by SEI film decomposition decreases under low SOC conditions, and it is less likely to cause local short circuit caused by lithium dendrite growth. At this point, the internal chemical reaction rate of the battery is controlled by the diffusion of lithium ions, and the exothermic process changes very uniformly. Therefore, it is difficult to obtain the threshold for thermal runaway from the analysis of the rate of temperature change.

claims.claim: Battery capacity changes very slowly and in most cases requires recalibration only once every several days or weeks, unlike SoC which varies rapidly.
claims.quote: the capacity changes very slowly, in most of cases it requires to be recalibrated once at several days or weeks.

claims.claim: Using a macro scale time-scale separation level L of 60 s instead of L = 1 s (dual EKF) reduced computation time by 47.3% in the multi-scale EKF implementation.
claims.quote: the run time is 2.341 s in this case; as a result, a 47.3% reduction in computation time when we use the multi-scale EKF comparing with the dual EKF.

claims.claim: When the macro scale L is set to the range of 120 s to 180 s, voltage and SoC estimation accuracy is much worse than with L = 60 s, although capacity prediction precision may occasionally be slightly better.
claims.quote: if the time scale separation level L is initialized to the ranges from 120 s to 180 s, we ﬁnd that the estimation accuracy of voltage and SoC is much worse than L = 60 s, although the prediction precision of capacity maybe a litter better some time

claims.claim: When macro scale is small and battery current is close to zero, the EKFh-based parameter estimate is prone to instability, which degrades prediction precision and performance.
claims.quote: when the macro scale is small, if the battery current is close to zero, the EKFh-based estimate is prone to sway from the variable voltage; as a result, it is hard to get stable estimation in battery parameter.

claims.claim: The maximum available capacity (discharge capacity) is a function of temperature and aging level, whereas the nominal capacity is not a function of temperature and aging level.
claims.quote: The nominal capacity is the maximum total electrical charge that can be stored in a cell. It is not a function of temperature and aging level. The maximum available capacity (discharge capacity) is the product of the nominal discharge current and discharge time with the nominal current... This indicates that the maximum available capacity is inﬂuenced by the operation condition and aging level of battery, thus the Q is a function of temperature and aging level.

claims.claim: With inaccurate initial capacity set to 20 Ah (true capacity 31.2 Ah) and no capacity estimator, both voltage and SoC estimation errors are much larger than cases where capacity is estimated concurrently.
claims.quote: From Fig. 16, we ﬁnd that both the voltage and SoC estimation error are much bigger than the above cases.

claims.claim: After stable estimation is achieved using the multi-scale EKF with L = 60 s, the peak estimation errors of voltage, SoC, and capacity are all less than ±2%.
claims.quote: all the peak errors of stable voltage, SoC and capacity estimation are less than ±2%

claims.claim: In a LixCoO2/LixC6 full cell system, the melting of the separator causes the cathode and anode materials to contact each other, and the resulting short circuit generates heat and reduces the thermal stability of the system.
claims.quote: The short circuit between the positive and negative electrode materials activated by the melting of separator. The short circuit generates a large amount of heat and reduces the thermal stability of the system.

claims.claim: For a full cell with mass ratios similar to commercial 18650 cells, the greatest contribution to heat generation is the disproportionation and decomposition of LixCoO2, and the second greatest contribution arises from the short circuit after separator melting.
claims.quote: When the mass ratio of the full cell is same as that of the commercial full cell, the greatest contribution to the heat generation of the full cell system is the disproportionation and decomposition of LixCoO2, and the second greatest contribution is arising from the short circuit.

claims.claim: The SEI layer on the negative electrode begins to break down at approximately 90–120°C, as the metastable components decompose in that temperature range.
claims.quote: The SEI layer mainly consists of stable (such as Li2CO3, LiF) and metastable components (such as polymers, (CH2OCOOLi)2, ROCOOLi and ROLi). The metastable components decompose at 90–120°C.

claims.claim: The polyethylene separator (Celgard 2400) melts with an onset temperature of 121.78°C, a peak temperature of 136.51°C, and a heat absorption of 233.19 J g⁻¹.
claims.quote: From results in Fig. 8 and Table 7, the separator shows an approximate endothermic peak with a heat generation of 233.19 J g⁻¹... Onset temp. (°C) 121.78, Peak temp. (°C) 136.51, Reaction heat (J g⁻¹) 233.19.

claims.claim: When the heating rate of a full cell is increased from 0.2°C min⁻¹ to 0.5°C min⁻¹, the onset and peak temperatures of reactions occurring after the short-circuit event are lowered, indicating that heating rate accelerates post-short-circuit reactions.
claims.quote: Peak d, e, f and g, which are after the short circuit peak (peak c), show lowered onset and peak temperatures. It can be deduced that the heating rate plays an important role in accelerating the reaction in the full cell system after the occurrence of the short circuit.

claims.claim: Reducing the electrolyte content in the LixCoO2-electrolyte system from a 1:2.2 to a 2.5:1 mass ratio eliminates the mild exothermic process near 60°C associated with thermally unstable substances formed on the cathode surface, suggesting that formation of these unstable substances is dependent on the presence of sufficient electrolyte.
claims.quote: Compared with Fig. 4, the mild exothermic process near 60°C does not show up when the mass ratio between LixCoO2 and electrolyte is 2.5:1. It can be speculated that the unstable substance formed on the cathode surface is relevant to the electrolyte. When the electrolyte is not too much, formation of the unstable substance is little.

claims.claim: ARC and VSP2 cannot detect endothermic reactions, making DSC and C80 microcalorimeter more suitable instruments when endothermic processes are present in the test object.
claims.quote: ARC and VSP2 cannot be applied to detect endothermic reaction, so DSC and C80 would be more suitable when endothermic process happen to the test object.

claims.claim: Side reactions contribute more than 80% of heat to thermal runaway in overcharge cases below 1.0 C charging rate, decreasing to 60% at 1.0 C and 40% at 2.0 C.
claims.quote: The accumulated heat of the cell is mainly from side reactions after Vi-p, and these reactions contribute more than 80% heat for thermal runaway in cases below 1.0 C and decreases to 60% in 1.0 C case and 40% in 2.0 C case

claims.claim: When the charging current rate exceeds 1.0 C, effective cooling or current cutoff methods must be applied within 2 minutes after the cell passes the inflection point voltage (Vi-p) to prevent thermal runaway.
claims.quote: When the current is upon 1.0 C, effective methods must be taken in less than 2 min to cut off the current and cool the lithium ion system as soon as the cells reach Vi-p.

claims.claim: The inflection point voltage (Vi-p) and maximum voltage (Vmax) both increase linearly with increasing current rate, described by Vi-p = 0.8|C| + 4.7 (r²=0.995) and Vmax = 0.8|C| + 5.0 (r²=0.998).
claims.quote: jV i(cid:4)pj ¼ 0:8jCj þ 4:7; r2 1 ¼ 0:995 jV maxj ¼ 0:8jCj þ 5:0; r2 2 ¼ 0:998

claims.claim: Overcharging with a galvanostatic-potentiostatic-galvanostatic (g-p-g-c) regime is more dangerous than a purely galvanostatic (g-c) regime, especially when the current rate is above 0.2 C, as indicated by lower onset temperatures in g-p-g-c cases.
claims.quote: overcharging with galvanostatic - potentiostatic - galvanostatic regime is more dangerous than that with galvanostatic way... It is worth noting that the Ttr is deﬁned as the temperature where the cell ruptured or ﬁred and get totally irreversible thermal runaway... overcharged cell with g-c regime is more stable than that in g-p-g-c way, especially when the current rate is above 0.2 C.

claims.claim: Li plating on the anode during overcharge is attributed to changes in the distance between cathode and anode caused by cell deformation from gas release, not to saturation of anode capacity.
claims.quote: it is assumed that the appearance of Li plating on anode is due to the changes of distance between the cathode and anode, instead of the satiation of anode capacity.

claims.claim: The exothermic reaction between delithiated cathode and electrolyte is accelerated above 60°C, at which point a large quantity of CO2 gas is evolved.
claims.quote: Cell surface temperature begins to rise more rapidly due to the exothermic reaction between delithiated cathode and the electrolyte. This reaction is accelerated above 60°C and a large quantity of CO2 gas is evolved.

claims.claim: The melting point of the separator was detected as 150°C, and when the cell internal temperature reaches the shutdown temperature of the separator (150–160°C), the overcharge current drops steeply and violent reactions lead to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The melting point of separator is detected as 150°C by thermal analysis test... When the cell internal temperature reaches the shutdown temperature of the separator (150–160°C), the overcharge current drops steeply and cell surface temperature leads to the violent reaction between the overcharged anode (deposited lithium) and electrolyte

claims.claim: Battery self-heating due to SEI film decomposition starts when temperature rises to 70–90 °C.
claims.quote: When the temperature rises up to 70–90 °C, the battery starts self-heating due to the decomposition of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film

claims.claim: The exothermic reactions between anode and electrolyte dominate battery heat generation before 250 °C, while cathode decomposition reactions begin to contribute after 250 °C.
claims.quote: the battery heat generation before 250 °C was dominated by the reactions on the anode/electrolyte interface, such as the decomposition of SEI film, and the reaction between anode active material and electrolyte. The reactions in sample Cat+An began to contribute to the total battery heat generation after the temperature reached 250 °C

claims.claim: Combustion of flammable gases always occurs outside the battery and has little influence on battery temperature response because the combustion energy heats the surroundings rather than the cell.
claims.quote: the combustion always occurs outside the batteries and has little inﬂuence on battery temperature response, as the combustion energy is not fully utilized to heat the cell, but heat the surroundings

claims.claim: Dahn's TR model failed to predict the maximum temperature because not all exothermic reactions were included in the model.
claims.quote: Dahn's model could predict the onset of battery TR well when comparing to the oven tests, but failed in the prediction of the maximum temperature, since not all the exothermic reactions were included in the model

claims.claim: The sharp experimental rise of battery temperature rate after TR onset is attributed to possible massive internal short circuit and violent reactions of all cell components in a short time, which are not captured by the component-level kinetics model.
claims.quote: The sharp rise of experimental battery temperature rate is due to possible massive internal short circuit and the violent reactions of all the cell components in a short time, which are difficult to simulate based on the investigations of cell components, and are thus not included in the TR model yet

claims.claim: For the 130 °C oven test, the battery did not reach thermal runaway, with Tmax reaching 185.84 °C (predicted) versus 179.43 °C (experimental), whereas at 150 °C the battery ran into thermal runaway with Tmax reaching approximately 857–860 °C.
claims.quote: The predicted Tmax during the 130 °C oven test reaches 185.84 °C, consistent with the experimental result (179.43 °C). For the 150 °C oven test, the battery ran into TR after heated to 150 °C and resting for 30 min… The maximum temperature Tmax reached 857.74 °C during the 150 °C oven test

claims.claim: Oxygen is released from cathode active material decomposition at around 250 °C, and this released oxygen reacts with anode active material, producing a distinct exothermic peak at around 290 °C.
claims.quote: Oxygen was released from the decomposition of cathode active material… The oxygen will react with the anode active material at elevated temperature, leading to a distinct exothermic peak at around 290 °C in the DSC proﬁle of sample Cat+An

claims.claim: The heat generation of sample Cat+Ele (428.1 J g⁻¹) was dominated by the self-decomposition of cathode active material, while the reaction between cathode active material and electrolyte produced limited heat, mainly due to solvent vaporization before oxygen release at around 250 °C.
claims.quote: the heat generation of sample Cat+Ele was dominated by the self-decomposition of cathode active material, while the reaction between cathode active material and electrolyte produced limited heat, mainly due to the vaporization of the solvents… before the release of oxygen from the cathode active material at around 250 °C

claims.claim: For cells with Li(NixCoyMnz)O2 cathode and carbon-based anode, the major heat source during thermal runaway is the redox reaction between the cathode and anode at high temperature, not the internal short circuit.
claims.quote: the major heat source during thermal runaway for cells with Li(NixCoyMnz)O2 cathode and carbon-based anode is the redox reaction between the cathode and anode at high temperature. In contrast to what is commonly thought, internal short circuits are responsible for very little of the total heat generated during thermal runaway

claims.claim: Internal short circuits contribute to triggering redox reactions after separator collapse but are not the major heat source during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: ISC did generate heat, but was not the major heat source that triggered TR... ISC is a key factor in triggering TR, but not the major heat source of TR.

claims.claim: T1, the onset temperature of abnormal heat generation, is observed at around 70–150 °C for all cell samples and corresponds to decomposition of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI), which usually initiates at 60–120 °C for commercial cells with carbon-based anodes.
claims.quote: T1 is observed at around 70–150 °C for all cell samples... For the commercial cells equipped with carbon-based anodes, T1 represents the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI), which usually initiates at 60–120 °C

claims.claim: High-temperature aging increases T1 due to thickening of the SEI layer, whereas low-temperature aging decreases T1 due to growth of lithium dendrites.
claims.quote: high-temperature aging led to an increase in T1, whereas low temperature aging decreased T1. The increase in T1 is caused by the thickening of SEI layer during high temperature cycling. The decrease is caused by the growth of lithium dendrites during cycling at a low temperature

claims.claim: Most T2 values are lower than 300 °C, which is the maximum temperature that most current commercial cells can withstand without thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Most of the T2 values were lower than 300 °C, which is the maximum temperature that most current commercial cells can withstand without TR.

claims.claim: T3 tends to be higher for cells with higher energy density, and T3 decreases linearly with decreasing state-of-charge for cells with NCM cathodes.
claims.quote: T3 tended to be higher for cells with higher energy density... T3 decreases linearly with decreasing SOC

claims.claim: For a normal NCM+LMO pouch cell (Cell Type A), Stage III redox reaction between cathode and anode contributes 74% of total heat generation during thermal runaway, Stage II ISC contributes 9%, and Stage I anode reactions contribute 17%.
claims.quote: Stage I... contributing 17% of the total heat released throughout the TR process... 9% of the total heat released during TR is due to ISC in Stage II... Stage III: The redox reaction between the cathode and the anode begins to release large amounts of heat. During this stage, 74% of total heat generation is released.

claims.claim: For cells with LFP cathode, the peak heat generation of the cathode system (SYSCA_ELE) is located at a temperature higher than 500 °C, which cannot be reached after the full energy of the anode system is released, so the cathode does not participate in the TR process, making LFP cells safer than NCM cells.
claims.quote: the peak of SYSCA_ELE for the LFP cell was located at a temperature higher than 500 °C, which cannot be reached after the full energy within SYSAN_ELE is released. Therefore SYSCA_ELE does not participate in the TR process for the LFP cells which are thus safer than the NCM cells.
