claims.claim: For cells with an NCM cathode, the heat generation power in DSC tests was more than one order of magnitude lower than in ARC tests between T2 and T3, indicating that anode reactions alone cannot explain the thermal runaway intensity.
claims.quote: for the NCM cells, the heat generation power in the DSC test was more than one order of magnitude lower than that in the ARC test... indicating that the reaction in SYSAN_ELE was not the major heat source from T2 to T3.

claims.claim: In a cell with dried electrolyte and separator removed (Sample III), ISC was continuous from the start of the test, but the heat generated by ISC could not trigger thermal runaway, and TR still occurred at T2 = 229.4 °C driven by the cathode-anode redox reaction.
claims.quote: ISC was continuous from the very beginning of the test, because the voltage was approximately zero from the start. The heat generated by ISC could not trigger TR according to the data... ISC did not contribute to the TR process that drove the cell from T2 to T3.

claims.claim: ISC lowered T2 by approximately 40 °C in a normal cell (T2 = 192.4 °C) compared to cells with dried electrolyte (T2 ≈ 230 °C), indicating ISC heats the cell to the threshold for the cathode-anode redox reaction.
claims.quote: T2 = 192.4 °C for the Battery Sample I is approximately 40 °C lower than that of Battery Samples II and III... ISC should be responsible for heating Battery Sample I from 192.4 °C to 230 °C. In other words, ISC is a key factor in triggering TR, but not the major heat source of TR.

claims.claim: For cells at 100% SOC, a threshold of 1 °C/s is recommended for detecting thermal runaway in cells with NCM cathode, whereas 10 °C/min is appropriate for cells with LFP cathode.
claims.quote: For cells at 100% SOC, 1 °C·s−1 is recommended for cells with an NCM cathode, whereas 10 °C·min−1 is appropriate for cells with LFP cathode.

claims.claim: The separator system (SYSSEP_ELE) generates little heat and absorbs heat when the separator melts, so it contributes little to the thermal runaway process.
claims.quote: The heat generation of SYSSEP_ELE contributes little to the TR process, as SYSSEP_ELE generates little but absorbs heat when separator melts

claims.claim: The maximum dT/dt during thermal runaway has a strong correlation with the energy density of the battery, and the logarithm of maximum dT/dt correlates linearly with battery SOC for NCM cathode cells.
claims.quote: The maximum dT·dt−1 that is reported at around T2 has a strong correlation with the energy density of the battery... The correlation of linearity also stands for the logarithm of the maximum dT·dt−1 and the battery SOC.

claims.claim: If the high-temperature aging causes the SEI layer to break rather than thicken, T1 will drop, indicating reduced thermal stability.
claims.quote: if the high temperature aging causes the SEI layer to break rather than thicken, the T1 will also drop

claims.claim: In the RLS-AHIF joint estimation method, erroneous initial parameters have a greater impact than sensor noises on the capacity estimation error.
claims.quote: the erroneous initial parameters have a greater impact than the sensor noises on the capacity estimation error.

claims.claim: The capacity convergence rate in dual estimation methods is very slow due to mutual coupling between capacity and SOC, and some methods take several hours to converge, making them unsuitable for electric vehicles operating in urban areas.
claims.quote: the capacity convergence rate is very slow, and some even take several hours, which is not suitable for electric vehicles operating in urban areas.

claims.claim: The system described by the state-space equations is locally observable if and only if the OCV derivative with respect to SOC (χ) is non-zero and the time constant τ is not infinity.
claims.quote: This matrix has full rank if and only if χ ≠ 0 and τ ≠ ∞. According to fact that the OCV is a monotonic function of SOC, and our previous assumption that OCV is not identical at the sampling intervals, χk will be hold in the discharge process.

claims.claim: When the SOC is in the range of 35–45%, χ (the first derivative of OCV to SOC) tends to 0, indicating the OCV-SOC curve changes very slowly in this interval, which may cause the estimated capacity accuracy to deteriorate.
claims.quote: χ tends to 0 at the range of 35–45% SOC, which indicates that the OCV-SOC curve change is very slow at this interval, which may cause the estimated capacity accuracy to deteriorate in this interval.

claims.claim: Voltage sensor noises, especially bias noise, have a larger influence than current sensor noises on the capacity estimation error in the RLS-AHIF method.
claims.quote: the voltage sensor noises, especially the bias noise, have a larger inﬂuence than current sensor noises on the capacity estimation error.

claims.claim: For an NMC Li-ion cell with an initial capacity error of 24% (Cn,0 = 1.6 Ah), the RLS-AHIF method achieves a maximum absolute capacity estimation error of 0.13 Ah (6.2%) with a convergence time of less than 2.5 minutes.
claims.quote: When the initial value of the capacity is set to 1.8 Ah, 1.7 Ah, 1.6 Ah, the corresponding estimated error of the capacity reaches 0.11 Ah (i.e., 5.2%), 0.12 Ah (i.e., 5.7%) and 0.13 Ah (i.e., 6.2%), respectively... even when the initial value error of the capacity reaches 24% (for Cn,0 = 1.6 Ah), the convergence time of the capacity estimation result is less than 2 min.

claims.claim: The RLS-AHIF method achieves convergence in less than 2 minutes under a 24% initial capacity error, compared to less than 100 minutes for DEKF and less than 10 minutes for multiscale DEKF with a 37% initial error.
claims.quote: RLS-AHIF: Initial capacity error 24%, Convergence time < 2 min, Estimation error < 6.5%. DEKF [17]: 33%, < 100 min, < 3.2%. Multiscale DEKF [17]: 33%, < 100 min, < 1.5%. Multiscale DEKF [18]: 37%, < 10 min, < 5%.

claims.claim: Current sensor bias and variance noises appear as a product with small terms such as χk−1ηiΔt, Ro,k, making the capacity estimation error caused by current sensor noises smaller than that caused by voltage sensor noises.
claims.quote: Current sensor bias and variance noises usually appear as a product with some very small terms such as χk−1ηiΔt, Ro,k, and (1 − ψk). Therefore, the capacity estimation error caused by current sensor noises is often smaller than that caused by voltage sensor noises.

claims.claim: For a typical ISC event in a LiCoO2/graphite battery, the local current during internal short circuit can be an order of magnitude higher than that for a regular 1C discharge.
claims.quote: the local current could be an order of magnitude higher than that for a regular 1C discharge, and the maximum heat release rate is around 1012 W/m3

claims.claim: The maximum heat release rate during a representative ISC scenario is around 10^12 W/m³.
claims.quote: the maximum heat release rate is around 1012 W/m3

claims.claim: The safety regime threshold for a LiCoO2/Graphite battery follows a power law relationship Q = E6.9 × dt^(−0.758), where Q is in W/m³ and dt is in seconds, with shorter durations making critical Q more sensitive to dt.
claims.quote: This curve is fitted to a power law form as Q = E6 9 dt−0.758 (Q in W/m3 and dt in s). With shorter duration, the critical Q is more sensitive to dt; while for longer duration time, such dependence becomes less and less sensitive.

claims.claim: For a LiCoO2/graphite battery with fixed heat source duration of 14 s, increasing the hot spot radius from 1.5 mm to 2 mm (a 33.3% increase) decreases the thermal runaway delay time by approximately 71.4%, from about 14 s to about 4 s.
claims.quote: compared to the base case with radius of 1.5 mm, the delay time of thermal runaway is substantially decreased to about 4 s, a 71.4% relative change, when the hot spot radius is increased to 2 mm by 33.3%

claims.claim: With a heat source radius of 1 mm (sufficiently small), thermal runaway does not occur even at a heat source intensity of Q = 1.18E9 W/m³ and duration dt = 14 s.
claims.quote: with a sufficiently small radius of the heat source, thermal runaway does not occur at all, as in the case of radius 1 mm in Fig. 3(a)

claims.claim: The sequence of the four thermal runaway reactions follows the order: SEI decomposition, negative electrode–electrolyte reaction, positive electrode–electrolyte reaction, and electrolyte decomposition.
claims.quote: the sequence of the four runaway reactions follow the order of SEI decomposition (SEI), negative electrode-electrolyte reaction (NE), positive electrode-electrolyte reaction (PE) and eventually electrolyte decomposition (ED)

claims.claim: The rate of the positive electrode–electrolyte reaction plays the most important role among chemical reactions in affecting the ignition delay time for thermal runaway, based on normalized sensitivity analysis.
claims.quote: For the chemical reactions, the rate of positive electrode-electrolyte reaction plays the most important role in affecting the delay time for thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Battery 3 (LiCoO2/Graphite) has a much broader safety region than Battery 1 (LiCoO2/Graphite with the same runaway kinetic parameters) because its thermal conductivity in-plane is higher by 36.8% and through-plane by 228.5%.
claims.quote: compared to Battery 1, thermal conductivity in and through plane is higher by 36.8% and 228.5% for Battery 3, respectively. This leads to a much broader safety region of Battery 3

claims.claim: During a typical ISC event, the maximum temperature in simulated batteries can increase by 30 °C within 2 ms, equivalent to a temperature rise rate of 15,000 °C/s.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature in these batteries can increase by 30 °C within 2 ms for this ISC event, equivalent to a temperature rise rate of 15000 °C/s.

claims.claim: In the ISC simulation, the relaxation time for the electrical field to achieve equilibrium is around 1 ms, regardless of battery material (LCO, NMC, or LFP).
claims.quote: Regardless the battery material, relaxation time for the field to achieve equilibrium is around 1 ms, and the maximum local heat release rate in the whole field can be as high as 1E12 W/m3.

claims.claim: Internal short circuits are responsible for very little (~10%) of the total heat generated during thermal runaway, although they contribute to triggering the redox reactions after the separator collapses.
claims.quote: Recent findings have shown that internal short circuits are responsible for very little (~10%) of the total heat generated during thermal runaway, although they contribute to triggering the redox reactions after the separator collapses

claims.claim: The thermal runaway boundary conditions are set as adiabatic because the cooling design on the battery surface can hardly be effective during the very fast runaway process, which has a delay time around 10 s.
claims.quote: since we are interested in very fast runaway process (with delay time around 10 s), during which the cooling design on the surface of a battery can hardly be effective, the thermal boundary conditions are hence intentionally set as adiabatic.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of thermal runaway (T2) obtained in the oven test is 48.1°C lower than that obtained in the adiabatic thermal runaway (ARC) test for a 24 Ah Li(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)O2/graphite pouch battery.
claims.quote: The onset temperature of thermal runaway (T2) obtained in the oven test is 48.1 ◦C lower than that obtained in the adiabatic thermal runaway test.

claims.claim: The maximum thermal runaway temperature (T3) measured at the battery surface is approximately half that measured at internal positions (center, JR_a, JR_b) under adiabatic test conditions.
claims.quote: T3 at the surface is approximately half that at the other positions. This is because part of the heat consumption in the heat transfer path occurs on the jelly roll and shell, which causes the temperature to decrease.

claims.claim: Under adiabatic test conditions, the maximum temperature rates at internal jelly roll positions (JR_a and JR_b) are approximately an order of magnitude higher than those at the center and surface positions.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature rates of JR_a and JR_b are approximately an order of magnitude higher than those at the center and surface.

claims.claim: In the oven test, when battery thickness increases from less than 5 cm to 10 cm, T2 at the center and JR_b decrease sharply by approximately 20.4°C and 25.1°C respectively, due to a change in thermal runaway trigger sequence from JR_b→JR_a to JR_a→JR_b.
claims.quote: When the thickness increases from 5 cm to 10 cm, T2 at the center and JR_b decrease sharply by approximately 20.4 ◦C and 25.1 ◦C, respectively, but their T2 values then increase slightly when the thickness exceeds 10 cm. This is due to the changes in the TR sequence from JR_b → JR_a to JR_a → JR_b when the thickness exceeds 5 cm.

claims.claim: When battery thickness reaches 10 cm in an oven test, the difference in T2 values between JR_a and the center exceeds 20% of the center value, caused by large-scale high-temperature heat transfer rather than internally generated heat.
claims.quote: The difference in T2 values between JR_a and the center is the largest, exceeding 20% of the center value. In fact, the values at the center and JR_b are not caused by the heat generated inside the battery, but instead result from the large-scale high-temperature heat transfer.

claims.claim: In the oven test, the sequence by which thermal runaway is triggered changes from center→surface to surface→center as oven temperature increases, causing T2 at the surface to increase and T2 at the center to decrease with increasing oven temperature.
claims.quote: The sequence by which TR was triggered inside the battery changed from center → surface to surface → center with the increase in oven temperature, leading to an increase in T2 at the surface and a decrease in T2 at the center.

claims.claim: Under adiabatic test conditions, T2 at different positions remains almost constant as battery thickness increases, whereas T3 increases at JR_a, JR_b, and the center but decreases at the surface as thickness increases.
claims.quote: T2 at different positions remains almost constant with the increase in thickness... As shown in Fig. 10(d), T3 increases at JR_a, JR_b, and the center as the thickness increases... The T3 at the surface shows a different trend and decreases by approximately 30 ◦C with the increase in battery thickness.

claims.claim: The critical temperature for mass gas generation in the model is set at 109°C, at which point gas thermal resistance is added to simulate external and internal battery swelling.
claims.quote: The critical temperature for mass gas generation is set as 109 ◦C in this model. When the core temperatures of the JR_a and JR_d reach the critical temperature of gas generation, Rgas_out is added to simulate the external swelling of the battery.

claims.claim: T1 (onset temperature of detectable self-heating) is defined as the temperature at which the battery temperature rate exceeds 0.02°C/min, while T2 is defined as the temperature when the battery temperature rate exceeds 20°C/min.
claims.quote: T1 is defined as the temperature at which the temperature rate exceeds 0.02 ◦C/min, while T2 is the temperature when the battery temperature rate exceeds 20 ◦C/min.

claims.claim: For oven tests with battery total thickness greater than 5 cm, the thermocouple should be placed inside the jelly roll near the battery surface to accurately measure characteristic temperatures, because T2 measured at the center can be driven below the true thermal runaway onset by high-temperature heat transfer from adjacent jelly rolls.
claims.quote: When the thickness is greater than 5 cm, the thermocouple should be placed inside the battery, and the actual position needs to be located in the jelly roll near the battery surface, such as JR_a in this study.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway occurs when the Thermal Runaway Number (TRN) exceeds 1, where TRN is a non-dimensional number combining heat generation slope with respect to temperature, cell thermal conductivity, cell radius, and the first eigenfunction root corresponding to the convective boundary condition.
claims.quote: it has been shown that thermal runaway occurs when the value of TRN exceeds 1.

claims.claim: For a low thermal conductivity cell (0.15 W/mK), increasing convective heat transfer coefficient h beyond 150 W/m2K does not further increase Tcritical, indicating a saturation limit for external cooling effectiveness.
claims.quote: for low thermal conductivity of 0.15 W/mK, Tcritical rises with increase in h, but becomes stable beyond h=150 W/m2K.

claims.claim: For a high thermal conductivity cell, Tcritical continues to increase even for convective heat transfer coefficient values above 150 W/m2K, unlike the low thermal conductivity case.
claims.quote: for a high thermal conductivity cell, Tcritical continues to increase even for higher h values.

claims.claim: There exists a lower limit of cell thermal conductivity k below which a Tcritical of 145°C or above is not achievable even with extremely aggressive thermal management such as liquid cooling.
claims.quote: there is a lower limit of the value of k, below which a Tcritical of 145°C or above is simply not possible, even with extremely aggressive thermal management, such as liquid cooling.

claims.claim: For a cell with thermal conductivity of 1.0 W/mK, the oven temperature must exceed 175°C to cause thermal runaway, compared to 160°C for the lowest thermal conductivity considered.
claims.quote: For a cell with thermal conductivity of 1.0W/mK, the oven temperatures needs to be above 175°C in order to cause thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The thermal conductivity of the thermal test cell was measured to be 0.20 W/mK, compared to 0.15 W/mK for an actual Li-ion cell, indicating similar thermal response.
claims.quote: The thermal conductivity of the thermal test cell has been measured to be similar to that of an actual Li-ion cell (0.20 W/mK vs. 0.15 W/mK), indicating that its thermal response is expected to be close to that of an actual Li-ion cell.

claims.claim: The TRN-based approach predicts a higher Tcritical than the Frank-Kamenetskii approach for the same cell thermal conductivity, with both models predicting increasing Tcritical as k increases but differing by a constant offset.
claims.quote: Both models predict an increase in Tcritical as k increases, and differ from each other only by a constant offset. In general, Tcritical for the TRN approach is greater than the Frank-Kamenetskii approach.

claims.claim: The Frank-Kamenetskii theory was originally derived for an isothermal outer surface boundary condition, which is not accurate in most realistic conditions, whereas the TRN approach exactly accounts for external cooling via a convective boundary condition.
claims.quote: while the TRN approach exactly accounts for external cooling by imposing the appropriate convective boundary condition on the outer surface [9], Frank-Kamenetskii theory was originally derived for an isothermal outer surface [38] – which is not accurate in most realistic conditions.

claims.claim: Increasing carbon content at the anode (Wc) causes Tcritical to decrease because larger Wc leads to more aggressive heat generation, triggering thermal runaway at lower temperatures.
claims.quote: This plot shows that Tcritical increases as Wc goes down. This occurs because the larger the value of Wc, the more aggressive heat generation is, and therefore, thermal runaway occurs at lower temperatures.

claims.claim: A natural convection cooling condition (low h) results in a Tcritical of approximately 125°C even for cells with very poor thermal conductivity, based on the multiple reaction model.
claims.quote: a low value of h, corresponding to natural convection cooling, leads to a Tcritical of 125°C, even if the cell thermal conductivity is very poor.

claims.claim: When the heat dissipation coefficient of an NCM lithium-ion battery reaches 10 W/(m²·K), thermal runaway can be effectively prevented under local heating conditions.
claims.quote: when the heat dissipation coefficient reaches 10 W/ (m2· K), the battery will not have thermal runaway. Therefore, improving heat dissipation capacity is an effective means to prevent thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Increasing the heat dissipation coefficient does not prevent the occurrence of pre-heat runaway reactions (SEI decomposition and negative-electrolyte reaction), but greatly reduces the heat of reaction.
claims.quote: the increase of the heat dissipation coefficient of the battery will not prevent the occurrence of the pre-heat runaway reaction, but the heat of reaction is greatly reduced.

claims.claim: Under local heating conditions with heat dissipation coefficient of 0.1 W/(m²·K), only SEI film decomposition and negative-electrolyte reaction occur; the cathode decomposition, binder reaction, and electrolyte decomposition reactions show no contribution to heat generation.
claims.quote: the SEI film decomposition and the negative-electrolyte reaction are the only two side reactions occur in the two tests. The other three side reactions show no contribution to the heat generation.

claims.claim: Under constant heating temperature, increasing the heating area increases the rate of material consumption and the rate of reaction, but does not change the maximum temperature rise or the type of side reactions.
claims.quote: under the condition of constant heating temperature, the rate of reaction increases with the increase of heating area, and the rate of material consumption increases... As the heating temperature and heating area of the battery increased, the consumption rate of the material increased, but the maximum temperature rise and the type of side reaction did not change.

claims.claim: As the heat dissipation coefficient of the battery surface increases, the temperature distribution uniformity of the separator layer improves, but at a heat dissipation coefficient of 10 W/(m²·K), the temperature distribution uniformity of the separator surface becomes worse compared to lower dissipation conditions.
claims.quote: when the heat dissipation coefficient of the battery reaches 10 W/ (m2·K), it can effectively control the occurrence of battery thermal runaway, but the temperature distribution uniformity of the separator surface becomes worse.

claims.claim: The SEI film decomposition reaction initiates at approximately 70°C (343.15 K), and the negative-electrolyte reaction initiates at approximately 120°C (393.15 K) in NCM lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: the initial temperature of SEI film decomposition reaction is about 70 ℃. The initial temperature of negative-electrolyte reaction is about 120 ℃.

claims.claim: The temperature jump point (onset of rapid temperature rise) during thermal runaway occurs at approximately above 400 K, consistent between experiment and simulation with a maximum temperature difference of less than 10 K and error less than 2.5%.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature difference between experiment and simulation is less than 10K, and the error is less than 2.5%. At the same time, we can see that the temperature jump point between experiment and simulation is basically the same, approximately more than 400 K.

claims.claim: The thermal abuse model used contains only five main thermal reactions, which causes the simulated maximum temperature to be lower than the experimental maximum temperature at high temperatures where thermal reactions become rapid and complex.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature of the battery in the experiment is two higher than that in the simulation, because the thermal reaction of the battery increases rapidly and is very complex with the high temperature, while the thermal abuse model contains only five main thermal reactions.

claims.claim: In an adiabatic environment, thermal runaway occurred at 177% SOC and required 6380 s, whereas in an ambient environment it occurred at 209% SOC and required 6910 s, confirming earlier and more dangerous thermal runaway under adiabatic conditions.
claims.quote: The time required to trigger thermal runaway was 6910 s in the ambient environment, whereas only 6380 s were required in the adiabatic environment. The SOC of the battery was 209% and 177% in the two environments, respectively, when thermal runaway occurred.

claims.claim: The apparent exothermic onset temperature T0 of a 150% SOC battery was 93.1 °C, which is 86.4 °C lower than the T0 of a 100% SOC battery at 179.5 °C, indicating significantly reduced thermal stability at higher SOC.
claims.quote: The T0 of LIBs with 100%, 110%, 120%, 130%, 140%, and 150% SOC were measured at 179.5, 129.1, 111.2, 110.6, 96.7, and 93.1 °C, respectively.

claims.claim: With increasing Li+ extraction depth from 100% to 150% SOC, the released heat from the cathode material increased from 760.4 to 1026.4 J/g and the second DSC heat generation peak shifted to lower temperatures, indicating greater reactivity at higher delithiation.
claims.quote: With increasing Li+ extraction depth from 100% to 150% SOC, the temperatures of the second heat generation peak apparently shifted to the lower side from 233 to 307 °C, and the released heat increased from 760.4 to 1026.4 J/g.

claims.claim: Lithium deposited on the anode during overcharge cycling does not participate in the next charge cycle but instead reacts with the electrolyte, resulting in lower coulombic efficiency and earlier thermal runaway at higher SOC.
claims.quote: The deposited lithium metal reacted with the electrolyte and was not available for the following cycles, resulting in a lower CE. The lithium deposited did not participate in the next charge cycle but instead reacted with electrolytes to release more heat, which led to a lower CE and earlier thermal runaway at a higher SOC.

claims.claim: Battery thickness increased monotonically with SOC after overcharge cycling, from an average of 6.24 mm at 100% SOC to 8.69 mm at 150% SOC, due to gas generation from electrolyte oxidation.
claims.quote: As the SOC increased, the batteries thickened. At a higher SOC, the overall volume of gas released from the batteries increased... Average thickness (mm): 100% SOC: 6.24, 110%: 6.76, 120%: 6.92, 130%: 7.06, 140%: 7.92, 150%: 8.69.

claims.claim: The maximum pressure Pmax during thermal runaway increased with SOC, from 664.1 psig at 100% SOC to 1545.4 psig at 150% SOC, attributed to greater gas volume released at higher SOC.
claims.quote: The Pmax of LIB with 100%, 110%, 120%, 130%, 140%, and 150% SOC were reported to be 664.1, 694.1, 916.5, 970.9, 1168.8, and 1545.4 psig, respectively. It can be speculated that the discrepancy of Pmax was caused by differences in the degree of the swell. At a high SOC, the overall volume of gas released from the batteries increased.

claims.claim: The jet process of a thermally abused 38 Ah Li(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)O2/graphite prismatic battery can be divided into four stages from the moment the vent disc pops out, with the first and second stages being instantaneous.
claims.quote: According to the manifestation of the jet behavior, the jet process could be divided into four stages from the moment the vent disc popped out. The first and second stages were instant.

claims.claim: At the third stage of the jet process, the gray jet had a small jet angle of approximately 20° with occasional sparks, in contrast to the large jet angle of approximately 80° at the first stage.
claims.quote: The gray jet had a small α of approximately 20° with occasional sparks... the lithium-ion battery ejected a white jet with a large jet angle α (~80°) at this stage.

claims.claim: Special phenomena including jet tilt, spark burst, and jet ignition may occur at the third stage of the thermal runaway jet process.
claims.quote: the jet seemed to be stable at the third stage, but special phenomena, such as the jet tilt, spark burst, and jet ignition, might appear.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature of the unignited jet at the third stage was similar in magnitude to the jet fire temperature reported in a previous study, indicating the thermal hazard of the unignited jet should not be underestimated.
claims.quote: The similarity between the maximum temperature of the jet in this study and that of the jet fire in a previous study indicated that the thermal hazard of the jet should also be emphasized.

claims.claim: The average jet velocity at the third stage, measured by tracking solid particles, was 8.3 m·s−1, and particle velocity may underestimate actual jet velocity because solid particle mass is non-negligible.
claims.quote: the red point represented the average velocity of the five particles captured, i.e. 8.3 m·s−1... However, the velocity of particles might be slower than the actual jet velocity because the mass of solid particles could not be ignored.

claims.claim: At the first stage of the jet process, two successive jet fronts were captured with exit velocities of approximately 55 m·s−1 at a height of 0.05 m and approximately 40 m·s−1 at a height of 0.09 m, with velocity decreasing with height due to air entrainment and energy conversion.
claims.quote: the velocity of the first jet at a height of 0.05 m was about 55 m·s−1, whereas the velocity of the second jet at a height of 0.09 m was about 40 m·s−1. The jet velocity decreased as height increased because of air entrainment and energy conversion.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway triggered by overcharging produced a severe jet fire due to additional electrical energy input, chemical and electrochemical heat generation, and oxygen production inside the battery, whereas thermal abuse heating alone did not ignite the jet.
claims.quote: in our pilot experiment, overcharging triggered the thermal runaway, and a severe jet fire was observed (Fig. 4), because of the additional electrical energy input, the chemical and electrochemical heat generation, and the oxygen production inside the battery.

claims.claim: The battery surface temperature increase experienced a delay of 5–10 s after venting, attributed to heat transfer between electrodes and the aluminum shell and to the Joule–Thomson expansion effect during venting.
claims.quote: its increase also experienced a delay of 5–10 s because of the venting action... The delayed temperature increase was mainly due to not only the heat transfer between the electrodes and the battery aluminum shell, but also the effect of the Joule–Thomson expansion by venting.

claims.claim: The internal temperature of the battery must exceed 250 °C at the third jet stage, causing the electrolyte to be in the gaseous phase.
claims.quote: The internal temperature of the battery must exceed 250 °C at the third jet stage, so the electrolyte is gaseous, as shown in Fig. 9b.

claims.claim: The jet tilt direction is determined by the relative position of the thermal runaway hot spot and the vent valve, with the jet tilting to the opposite side of the thermal runaway position.
claims.quote: The relative position of the hot spot and the vent valve determined that the jet tilted to the opposite side of the thermal runaway position.

claims.claim: For LTO anode cells, there is no SEI layer protection because the lithiation potential of LTO limits reduction of electrolyte components, so the SEI exponential term is set to 1.
claims.quote: It should be noted that there is no SEI layer protection on the surface of LTO, since its lithiation potential limits reduction of electrolyte components. Thus the value of this term is 1 for LTO anode.

claims.claim: At a heating power of 500 W/m², the onset temperature of thermal runaway for the LTO anode cell (437 K) is higher than for the graphite anode cell (407 K), and the onset time of TR for the graphite anode cell is 650 s earlier than for the LTO anode cell.
claims.quote: when the heating power is 500 W m⁻², the onset temperature of TR for LTO cell is 437 K, which is higher than that for graphite cell (407 K). It can also be found from Fig. 7(a) that the onset time of TR for the cell with graphite anode is 650 s earlier than that of the cells with LTO anode.

claims.claim: The peak temperature of thermal runaway for the LTO anode cell (705 K) is much lower than that for the graphite anode cell (917 K) because the reaction between graphite anode and electrolyte releases more enthalpy due to the higher energy density of lithiated graphite compared to lithiated LTO.
claims.quote: the peak temperature of TR for LTO cell is 705 K, which is much lower than that of 917 K for the cell with graphite anode. The above differences are believed to be due to the SEI protection... it is because the reaction between graphite anode and electrolyte releases more enthalpy due to the higher energy density of lithiated graphite compared to the lithiated LTO anode.

claims.claim: At a heating power of 500 W/m², the TR onset temperatures for four cathode materials are 407 K (LCO), 437 K (NCA), 457 K (NCM), and 475 K (LFP), giving a thermal stability order of LFP > NCM > NCA > LCO.
claims.quote: Fig. 8(a) shows that the TR onset temperatures are 407 K, 437 K, 457 K and 475 K for LCO cell, NCA cell, NCM cell and LFP cell, respectively... the thermal stability order of the LIBs with different cathode materials can be ranked as: LFP cell > NCA cell > NCM cell > LCO cell.

claims.claim: The peak TR temperatures are approximately 780 K (LCO), 929 K (NCA), 815 K (NCM), and 717 K (LFP), giving a thermal safety order of LFP > LCO > NCM > NCA, with NCA releasing the most enthalpy.
claims.quote: the peak temperatures are about 780 K, 929 K, 815 K and 717 K for LCO cell, NCA cell, NCM cell and LFP cell respectively. The predictions indicate that the decomposition of NCA cathode releases the most enthalpy compared to others... Hence the thermal safety order of the LIBs with different cathode is: LFP cell > LCO cell > NCA cell > NCM cell.

claims.claim: The thermal safety and thermal stability rankings of cathode materials are consistent between oven tests and local heating conditions, indicating that the change of thermal abuse method does not affect the relative safety and stability rankings.
claims.quote: the thermal safety and thermal stability of the above cathode materials are consistent with previous results measured under oven tests, which means the change of thermal abuse measures does not impact the thermal safety and thermal stability ranks of LIBs, regardless of oven tests or local heating.

claims.claim: Increasing the separator melting point improves the onset temperature and onset time of thermal runaway but does not affect peak temperature, because different separator melting points have little impact on the release of electrical and chemical energy stored inside the cell once TR occurs.
claims.quote: the onset temperature increases with the increasing melting point of separator... Correspondingly, the onset time of TR also increases with the increasing melting point of separator. In addition, it is noteworthy that the peak temperature keeps the same for the cell with various separators, which indicates the different melting points of separator materials have little impact on the release of electrical and chemical energy stored inside the cell once TR occurs.

claims.claim: When the separator melting point increases from 408 K (PE) to 438 K (PP), the TR onset time is delayed by 215 s, but when it increases from 438 K to 473 K (ceramic), the onset time is only delayed by 53 s, indicating diminishing returns above a certain melting point threshold.
claims.quote: as the melting point of cell separator increases from 408 K to 438 K (from PE to PP), the time of initiating TR reduces by 215 s. However, as the melting point increases from 438 K to 473 K, the onset time is only reduced by 53 s... when the melting point of separator is up to a certain value, the effects of relief may be no longer remarkable.

claims.claim: For the ceramic coated separator, the volume fraction of SEI layer and anode reduces by 12.33% and 30.07% before TR, indicating that side-reaction heat generation contributes to triggering TR, whereas for PE separator only 4.64% and 0.76% reduction occurs, meaning TR is mainly triggered by external heating.
claims.quote: the volume fraction of SEI layer and anode reduces about 4.64% and 0.76% before TR for PE separator, which indicates the heat triggering TR mainly comes from external heating. However, the volume fraction of SEI layer and anode reduces about 12.33% and 30.07% before TR for the ceramic coated separator, which indicates the decomposition of SEI layer and anode also provides part of the heat for the triggering TR.

claims.claim: Even if the separator melting point is raised to an extremely high temperature, thermal runaway still occurs due to decomposition reactions of electrolyte and binder.
claims.quote: even if the melting point of cell separator raises to an extremely high temperature, TR also takes place due to the decomposition reaction of electrolyte and binder.

claims.claim: The variety of electrolyte has limited effect on thermal safety and stability compared to other battery materials; the thermal safety and stability order is LiPF6/EC:DEC > LiPF6/PC:DEC > LiPF6/EC:DMC > LiPF6/PC:DMC.
claims.quote: compared to the study of previous sections, it can be found that the change of electrolyte has little significant effect on the peak temperature and onset time of TR, which means it does not play a dominant role in determining thermal behavior of cells under local heating.

claims.claim: Heating near the bottom of the cell triggers thermal runaway earlier than heating at the end or middle positions; at 6000 W/m² the onset times are 200 s (bottom), 272 s (end), and 536 s (middle).
claims.quote: With the heating power of 6000 W m⁻², the onset time of TR is 200 s, 272 s, 536 s at the position of bottom, end and middle respectively.

claims.claim: Heating at the bottom of the cell leads to earlier TR because the lower average surface temperature results in lower heat loss compared to other positions, causing a higher temperature rise rate, and the longer heat transfer path causes heat to accumulate more easily at the cell core.
claims.quote: heating on the bottom of the cell shows lower average surface temperature than heating on the end and middle, which makes the heat loss lower than other heating positions in heating stage. In this case, the temperature rise rate is largest, causing TR to occur sooner than other heating positions. In addition, owing to the longer transfer path, the heat is easier to accumulate making the cell core temperature higher when heating on the bottom of the cell.

claims.claim: When the total heating power applied to the cell surface is less than 0.75 W, the cell stays in steady-state and thermal runaway does not occur; the critical total power threshold that can trigger TR is between 0.75 W and 1.0 W.
claims.quote: the total power (heating area S × heating power q) applied to the cells of TR is larger than 1.0 W, and the minimum of total power that does not trigger TR is 0.75 W, which means the critical value of the total power that can trigger TR may be between 0.75 W and 1.0 W.

claims.claim: Under local heating conditions, peak temperature is little influenced by varying heating conditions (area and power), in contrast to oven tests where peak temperature increases with oven temperature because only side-reactions with onset temperatures below the oven temperature can proceed.
claims.quote: the peak temperature is little influenced by the various heating conditions in this study while the peak temperature increases with the oven temperature under oven test. The reason may lie in that only the side-reactions whose onset temperature is lower than the oven temperature can proceed for the cell under oven test.

claims.claim: At ambient temperature of 300 K with heating power of 1000 W/m² and heating size of 15 cm², thermal runaway occurs for cells cooled with air velocities of 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 2.5 m/s at onset times of 552 s, 587 s, 832 s, and 1286 s respectively, but not for the cell cooled at 5 m/s.
claims.quote: When the ambient temperature increases to 300 K, TR is observed for the cell cooled with the air velocity of 0.01, 0.1, 1 and 2.5 m s⁻¹ at 552 s, 587 s 832 s and 1286 s.

claims.claim: At ambient temperature of 320 K with heating power of 1000 W/m² and heating size of 15 cm², only the cell cooled with an air velocity of 5 m/s can stay in steady-state condition.
claims.quote: When the ambient temperature increases to 320 K, only the cell cooled with the air velocity of 5 m s⁻¹ can stay in steady-state condition.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway can be prevented when the heat dissipation coefficient of the battery reaches 10 W/m²·K, as reported for NCM cells under local heating on the cathode current collector.
claims.quote: They reported that TR could be prevented when the heat dissipation coefficient of the battery reached 10 W m⁻² K⁻¹.

claims.claim: For 243 Ah LiFePO4/graphite batteries at 100% SOC, the peak heat release rate reached 88.6 kW and the total heat release reached 19530.6 kJ during ignited thermal abuse tests.
claims.quote: The HRR and THR can reach 88.6 kW and 19530.6 kJ, respectively.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of thermal runaway decreased with increasing SOC, measuring 227.5°C for 50% SOC and 194.4°C for 100% SOC batteries.
claims.quote: The TR occurred with onset temperature of 227.5 and 194.4 °C for 50% and 100% SOC batteries, respectively. The onset time and temperature of TR show a large decrease with increasing SOC.

claims.claim: Flaming combustion accelerated the time from safety valve opening to thermal runaway by 336 s (23%) for 50% SOC and 284 s (37%) for 100% SOC batteries, compared to the unignited condition.
claims.quote: The time required from SV to TR for 50% and 100% SOC is advanced by 336 s (23%) and 284 s (37%) for the case 1, respectively.

claims.claim: The characteristic temperatures of safety valve opening, voltage drop, and thermal runaway onset were comparable between ignited and unignited conditions at the same SOC, indicating that characteristic temperatures are determined by inner reactions rather than external flame.
claims.quote: The onset temperature of SV, VD and TR are comparable for both case 1 and case 2 cells with the same SOCs. These results indicate that the characteristic temperature is determined by the inner reactions, and the flaming combustion accelerates the progress of TR.

claims.claim: In a 50 m³ room scenario, the estimated HF concentration of 162 mg/m³ far exceeds the IDLH limit of 24.6 mg/m³, while CO concentration can reach 0.25 g/m³, exceeding the 0.23 g/m³ threshold at which individuals lose judgment after up to 2 hours of exposure.
claims.quote: HF has the largest toxicity impacts, which far exceeds the IDLH value of 24.6 mg/m3, while other gases are relatively less toxic... The CO concentration can reach 0.25 g/m3, exceeding the value of 0.23 g/m3 when individuals will lose judgment for up to 2 h exposure.

claims.claim: No jet fire occurred for 0% SOC LiFePO4 batteries during ignited thermal abuse tests, whereas jet fire events were observed for 50% and 100% SOC batteries.
claims.quote: Note that no jet fire occurred under 0% SOC cells and it experienced three stages.

claims.claim: The average effective heat of combustion (AEHC) remained approximately constant across 0%, 50%, and 100% SOC conditions (13.7, 14.3, and 17.2 kJ/g respectively), while total heat release increased with SOC.
claims.quote: The THR shows an increase trend with increasing SOC, while the AEHC approximately keeps constant with different SOCs. The AEHC is as a function of fire dynamics and is affected by the combustion efficiency or ventilation rate.

claims.claim: The quantities of HCl and NH3 emitted during combustion showed no SOC dependence and remained low, whereas HF, NO, and SO2 productions increased with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: the HF, NO and SO2 productions increase with the increasing SOC, while the quantities of HCl and NH3 have no SOC dependence and remain low.

claims.claim: The strong covalent P-O bonds in LiFePO4 inhibit oxygen evolution, limiting heat production and reducing the possibility of self-ignition, but LFP cells still present high fire risk with external ignition sources and produce more unignited jet flow than other Li-ion chemistries.
claims.quote: For LFP cells, the strong covalent P-O bonds inhibit the evolution of oxygen and vastly limit the amount of heat produced that can in turn reduce the possibility of self-ignition and the severity of LIBs fire... there still exists high fire risks with external ignition sources and more unignited jet flow occurs over that of other Li-ion chemistries.

claims.claim: The fire growth index (FGI) for 100% SOC batteries was 1.3 kW/m²s compared to 0.11 kW/m²s for 50% SOC batteries, indicating a much higher fire growth rate at full charge.
claims.quote: The resulting value of FGI is 0.11 and 1.3 kW/m2s, indicating that the fire growth rate of 100% SOC batteries is much higher than that of 50% SOC batteries.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature during LIB thermal runaway was 1262°C, while the maximum temperature during ICE combustion was 1829°C, making the ICE combustion maximum temperature 1.4 times that of the LIB thermal runaway process.
claims.quote: The maximum temperatures were 1262 °C and 1829 °C for the LIB thermal runaway process and the ICE combustion process, respectively. The maximum temperature of the latter was 1.4 times that of the former.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate (dT/dt)max during LIB thermal runaway was 15,660°C/min, while during ICE combustion it was 43,383,762°C/min, making the ICE value 2,770 times that of the LIB process.
claims.quote: In the current study, (dT/dt)max were 15,660 °C / min during the LIB thermal runaway process and 43,383,762 °C / min during the ICE combustion process, respectively. The latter was 2,770 times that of the former.

claims.claim: The time interval from the sharp temperature rise to maximum temperature was 36.1 seconds for the LIB thermal runaway process and 2.75 ms for the ICE combustion process, making the LIB interval 13,127 times longer than the ICE interval.
claims.quote: The intervals from the time at which temperature increased sharply to the time at which the maximum temperature appeared were 36.1 s and 2.75 ms for LIB thermal runaway process and ICE combustion process, separately. The time interval of the former was 13,127 times that of the latter.

claims.claim: Tsa is defined as the lowest temperature at which automatic and continuous accelerated reactions can continue after abuse is stopped; at Tsa the cell heat generation rate equals the heat dissipation rate.
claims.quote: the lowest temperature at which the automatic and continuous accelerated reactions can continue when the abuse is stopped is called the thermal runaway self-accelerated chemical reaction temperature (Tsa) in this study... the cell heat generation rate is equal to the heat dissipation rate at Tsa

claims.claim: When cell temperature is lower than Tsa after abuse is stopped and the increasing slope of the self-heating rate curve is greater than that of the heat dissipation curve, heat accumulates and thermal runaway results.
claims.quote: When the cell temperature is lower than Tsa after the abuse is stopped, and the increasing slope of self-heating rate curve is greater than that of the heat dissipation curve, heat can be accumulated, resulting in increased cell temperature and thermal runaway, as shown in case 3 of Fig. 10.

claims.claim: Anodic SEI has higher reactivity and undergoes exothermic physical and chemical reactions in the electrolyte earlier than other cell materials at approximately 90–120°C, followed by lithiated graphite anode and electrolyte reactions, and finally cathode material decomposition with oxygen generation.
claims.quote: anodic SEI has higher reactivity and involves in physical and chemical reactions exothermally in the electrolyte earlier than other material of the cell at about 90–120 °C, followed by the lithiated (charged) graphite anode and electrolyte reacting exothermally, and finally cathode material decomposing with generation of oxygen which reacted with the electrolyte exothermally.

claims.claim: The decomposition temperature of cathode materials increases in the order LCO < NCA < NCM < LMO < LFP, with higher decomposition temperature corresponding to lower reactivity and higher stability.
claims.quote: the decomposition temperature of the cathode material was increasing in the following order: LCO < NCA < NCM < LMO < LFP. Therefore, it is believed that the higher decomposition temperature, the lower reactivity of the cathode materials with higher stability.

claims.claim: LFP cathode material does not release oxygen during decomposition, which is identified as one of the most important reasons LFP batteries are safer than other chemistries, because the absence of oxygen prevents increases in the reactivity of other cell materials.
claims.quote: The LFP decomposition reaction takes place without oxygen release. So one of the most important reasons that LFP battery is safer than others is LFP cathode material will not make the reactivity of other material inside the cell higher.

claims.claim: External thermal management can extend the slow reaction period by lowering the internal cell temperature but cannot control the rapid reaction period due to limited internal temperature control.
claims.quote: This method can extend the slow reaction period by lowering the internal temperature of the cell, but it cannot control the rapid reaction period. This is because the internal temperature control is limited.

claims.claim: To prevent LIB re-ignition after a fire, the cell temperature must be lowered to below Tsa.
claims.quote: After the cell catches fire, its temperature should be lowered to Tsa to prevent it from re-igniting.

claims.claim: The stability of cathode materials depends strongly on the Li content (state of charge), with lower SOC corresponding to lower reactivity and higher stability of cathode materials.
claims.quote: Li-ions stabilize the cathode material structure, a stability decrease was observed with the removal of Li from the layered oxides, that was, with rising state-of-charge (SOC). Therefore, it is believed that the lower SOC, the lower reactivity of the cathode materials with higher stability.

claims.claim: The main reason for uncontrolled LIB thermal runaway is that the internal cell boundaries are not controlled effectively.
claims.quote: Based on the ICE combustion theory, it can be concluded that the main reason for the un-controlled LIB thermal runaway process is that the internal cell boundaries are not controlled effectively.

claims.claim: Peak power is defined as the maximum power that can be maintained continuously for T seconds without violating the preset operational design limits on the cell's current, voltage, SOC, or power, based on the present battery conditions.
claims.quote: Peak power: based on the present battery conditions, the maximum power that can be maintained continuously for T seconds without violating the preset operational design limits on the cell's current, voltage, SOC, or power.

claims.claim: Using a single constant peak power as the power limit can cause over-voltage and over-current conditions, or the battery may fail to provide expected power due to self-protection, both of which can result in danger to vehicles or passengers.
claims.quote: if a single constant peak power is used as the power limit, certain abnormal situations, such as over-voltage and over-current conditions, may occur to the battery, or the battery may not be able to provide the vehicle with the expected power because of the self-protection of the battery system. Both of these cases can result in danger to the vehicles or passengers

claims.claim: At normal temperature (30°C), the RMS voltage estimation error of the proposed DEKF-based estimator is less than 3 mV over the entire operating range and less than 2 mV excluding the lowest 10% SOC.
claims.quote: At a normal temperature (Test 1 and Test 3), the root mean square (RMS) of the voltage estimation error is less than 3 mV over the entire operating range and less than 2 mV in the operating range excluding the lowest 10% SOC

claims.claim: At low temperature (−10°C), the RMS voltage estimation error of the proposed DEKF-based estimator is less than 7 mV over the entire operating range, which is worse than at normal temperature.
claims.quote: At a lower temperature (Test 2), the RMS voltage error is less than 7 mV over the entire operating range.

claims.claim: At low temperature (−10°C) with larger internal resistance, the battery voltage can reach its limit value before the current limit is reached, causing the entire pulse process to be restricted by the constant voltage condition.
claims.quote: Cases (c) and (d) were performed under the lower temperature test (−10°C), and the battery's internal resistance was larger. In case (c), the voltage reached its limit value before the current did, and the entire process was restricted by the constant voltage condition.

claims.claim: The ohmic resistance RO noticeably increases with the battery aging process, as observed by comparing estimated parameters from a cell with SOH=100% versus SOH=79%.
claims.quote: The value of RO noticeably increases with the battery aging process.

claims.claim: At all validation points across different temperature and aging conditions, both the relative error of peak power prediction and the SOP prediction error are less than 5%.
claims.quote: At all the validation points, both the relative error of the peak power prediction and the SOP prediction error are less than 5%.

claims.claim: A battery model initialized with static parameters identified in ideal laboratory conditions will not be able to get an accurate estimation in various actual applications.
claims.quote: a battery model initialized with static parameters which are identified in ideal laboratory conditions will not be able to get an accurate estimation in various actual applications.

claims.claim: In cyclic-state-estimation methods, the estimated battery capacity fluctuates around an assumed constant capacity rather than converging to its actual value, because the actual SOC value needed in the SOH observer is estimated based on an assumed constant capacity.
claims.quote: The estimated battery capacity will fluctuate around the assumed constant capacity, rather than converge to its actual value. Because the actual capacity is estimated according to a closed-loop based on a quantitative relationship of SOC error. However, the actual SOC value needed in the SOH observer is estimated based on an assumed constant capacity.

claims.claim: Under the proposed parameter adaptive sliding mode observer method, the absolute error of SOC estimation is less than 2% when verified through the UDDS driving cycle.
claims.quote: the absolute error of state of charge is less than 2%

claims.claim: The estimated battery capacity converges to the actual value within approximately 2500 seconds and the estimated battery capacity error rate is less than 3%, as verified through the UDDS driving cycle.
claims.quote: In about 2500 seconds, the estimated capacity converges to the actual one and tends to be stable with few minor fluctuations... the estimated lithium-ion battery capacity could converge to the actual value in 2500s and the estimated battery capacity error rate was less than 3%.

claims.claim: When the initial SOC error is less than 20% (typical self-discharge condition), the convergence time of the proposed SOC observer should be less than 400 seconds.
claims.quote: Under normal conditions, the initial SOC error of a battery is from self-discharge. In general, it is much smaller than 20%. So the convergence time should be less than 400 seconds generally.

claims.claim: The SOC estimation MAE of the proposed parameter adaptive model method is -0.0031% compared to -0.0133% for the traditional static model under dynamic current conditions, indicating lower error with the adaptive approach.
claims.quote: Parameter adaptive model (%) MAE -0.0031 ... Traditional static model (%) MAE -0.0133

claims.claim: For cell 'A' (NCA/Graphite), the on-set temperature of thermal runaway decreased from 147°C at SOC 0% to 79°C at SOC 100%, as measured by ARC.
claims.quote: on-set temperature (OST) decreased from 147ºC for the discharged cell 'A' to 79ºC for fully charged one (Table 3)

claims.claim: For cell 'A' (NCA/Graphite), the maximal self-heat rate rose by three orders of magnitude for cells with SOC 50% and 100% compared to SOC 0%.
claims.quote: maximal self-heat rate rose on three orders of magnitude for the cells with SOC 50% and 100% in comparison with one at SOC 0%

claims.claim: For cell 'A' (NCA/Graphite), the final adiabatic temperature increased from 416°C at SOC 0% to 790°C at SOC 100%.
claims.quote: final adiabatic temperature increased from 416ºC to 790ºC for cells with SOC 0% and 100%, respectively

claims.claim: The total measured energy of decomposition of the LP30 electrolyte is 396 J·g⁻¹, with a well-pronounced exothermic peak within the 250–300°C interval caused by combustion of electrolyte components.
claims.quote: The total measured energy of decomposition of the LP30 electrolyte is 396 J·g-1... a well pronounced exothermic peak within the 250-300ºC interval caused by the combustion of the electrolyte components

claims.claim: The addition of LP30 electrolyte to separator 'A' delayed thermal decomposition by 80°C but increased the enthalpy of the exothermic reaction more than two times, from 28 J·g⁻¹ to 63 J·g⁻¹.
claims.quote: with the addition of LP30 electrolyte the thermal decomposition of the separator 'A' was delayed by 80ºC but the enthalpy of the exothermic reaction increased more than two times up to 63 J·g-1

claims.claim: The fully charged NCA cathode 'A' (SOC 100%) in contact with electrolyte underwent a violent exothermic reaction with a total heat release of 2000 J·g⁻¹, attributed to collapse of the delithiated crystalline structure and release of oxygen that supports electrolyte combustion.
claims.quote: The total amount of the released heat release during the decomposition of the 'cathode 'A' SOC 100% - electrolyte' system is 2000 J·g-1.

claims.claim: The fully charged graphite anode 'A' (SOC 100%) in contact with electrolyte released an overall enthalpy of 1880 J·g⁻¹, compared to 81 J·g⁻¹ for the discharged anode 'A' in contact with electrolyte.
claims.quote: The overall enthalpy released during heating of the 'fully charged anode 'A' - electrolyte' system is 1880 J·g-1... The overall exothermic reaction enthalpies released during heating of the discharged anode and electrolyte is 81 J·g-1.

claims.claim: For cell 'B' (LFP/Graphite), the on-set temperature rose from 97–98°C for semi- and fully charged cells to 218°C for the cell at SOC 0%.
claims.quote: on-set temperature rose from 218 ºC for the cell at SOC 0% to 97-98ºC for semi and fully charged cells

claims.claim: For cell 'B' (LFP/Graphite), all investigated cells reached a similar final adiabatic temperature in the 420–453°C range regardless of SOC.
claims.quote: all investigated 'B' cells reached quite similar final adiabatic temperature in the 420-453ºC range

claims.claim: For the LFP cathode 'B', increasing SOC from 0% to 100% lowered the on-set temperature of thermal decomposition by 26°C in the presence of electrolyte.
claims.quote: On-set temperature of the cathode 'B' thermal decomposition becomes lower on 26ºC when SOC increases from 0% to 100%.

claims.claim: The relatively low impact of LFP cathode SOC on thermal instability is caused by the intrinsic structural stability of LiFePO4 and FePO4 phases due to the strength of P-O covalent bonds, which significantly reduces the rate of oxygen release and suppresses electrolyte combustion.
claims.quote: Relatively low impact of the LFP cathode´s SOC is caused by intrinsic structural stability of the LiFePO4 and FePO4 phases due to strength of P-O covalent bonds which significantly reduce the rate of oxygen release, thereby suppressing the combustion of the liquid electrolyte

claims.claim: For cells 'B' (LFP/Graphite) at SOC 50% and 100%, the graphite anode generates 3–4 times more heat than the LFP-based cathode.
claims.quote: The graphite anode generates 3-4 times more heat than the LFP based cathode.

claims.claim: For cell 'C' (NCA/LTO), the on-set temperature for the fully discharged cell was 45°C higher than for charged cells.
claims.quote: On-set temperature for fully discharged cell 'C' is on 45ºC higher that for charged cells

claims.claim: The uncharged LTO anode 'C' showed no significant thermal activity within the studied temperature range due to the high thermal stability of LTO ceramic.
claims.quote: The uncharged anode 'C' showed no significant thermal activity due to high thermal stability of LTO ceramic within studied temperature range

claims.claim: For the NCA cathode 'C', the on-set temperature of thermal decomposition in contact with electrolyte decreased by 64°C when SOC increased from 0% to 50%, and by a further 8°C when SOC rose from 50% to 100%.
claims.quote: On-set temperature of the cathode thermal decomposition becomes lower on 64ºC when state of charge increased up to 50%, and then decreases on 8ºC more when SOC rose up to 100%.

claims.claim: Cell 'B' (LFP/Graphite) demonstrated significantly lower self-heating rate compared to cell 'C' (NCA/LTO) and especially cell 'A' (NCA/Graphite) at any state of charge.
claims.quote: The cell 'B' demonstrated significantly lower self-heating rate in comparison with cell 'C' and, especially, cell 'A' at any state of charge

claims.claim: Despite the charged graphite anode in cell 'B' decomposing with similar enthalpy to the NCA cathode in cell 'C', cell 'B' demonstrated a much lower self-heating rate, indicating that the thermal stability of the positive electrode plays a more significant role than the negative electrode for whole-battery thermal behaviour.
claims.quote: despite the fact that charged graphite anode in cell 'B' decomposes with similar enthalpy of the NCA cathode in cell 'C', cell 'B' demonstrated much lower self-heating rate. These data indicates that the thermal stability of the positive electrode plays more significant role than the negative one for the whole battery thermal behaviour.

claims.claim: The thermal stability of harvested separators does not play a crucial role in the thermal runaway process of the investigated lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: it seems thermal stability of the harvested separators does not play a crucial role on thermal runaway process of the investigated lithium ion batteries.

claims.claim: All three investigated lithium-ion cells (NCA/Graphite, LFP/Graphite, NCA/LTO) underwent thermal runaway with explosion and carbonization independently of state of charge and cell chemistry, reaching EUCAR hazard level 7.
claims.quote: All cells studied by ARC method have the highest EUCAR hazard level 7 because underwent thermal runaway independently of the state of charge and cell chemistry.

claims.claim: Graphite-based anodes are thermally unstable in the 90–120°C range due to decomposition of metastable components of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer.
claims.quote: Graphite-based anode is thermally unstable in the 90-120ºC range due to the decomposition of metastable components of the solid electrolyte layer (SEI)

claims.claim: Increasing electrode loading in cell 'A', considered as a solution for energy density improvement, negatively affects lithium-ion battery safety.
claims.quote: it is important to note that the increasing of electrode loading (cell 'A'), considered as a solution for energy density improvement, negatively affects LIB safety.

claims.claim: There is no direct correlation between the on-set decomposition temperature of separated electrodes and separators in DSC tests and the whole-cell on-set temperature in ARC tests.
claims.quote: It seems there is not direct correlation of the on-set decomposition temperature of the separated electrodes and separators in DSC tests (Fig. 5a) with the whole cell OST in ARC tests (Fig. 6a).

claims.claim: The second-order RC equivalent circuit model neglects solid-state diffusion of the lithium-ion battery, and the ideal capacitor cannot accurately simulate the double-layer effect.
claims.quote: The parallel RC circuits simulate the middle frequency region of the impedance spectrogram. But the solid-state diffusion of the lithium-ion battery is neglected, and the ideal capacitor cannot accurately simulate the double-layer effect.

claims.claim: A multiple-order RC equivalent circuit results in high complexity, difficult parameter identification, and long simulation time.
claims.quote: a multiple-order RC equivalent circuit is needed to improve the model accuracy. However, a multiple-order equivalent circuit can result in high complexity, difficult parameter identification, and long simulation time.

claims.claim: The electrochemical impedance spectrogram of a lithium-ion battery cannot accurately reflect the dynamic response of the battery at very low operating temperatures such as -30°C.
claims.quote: It can reflect the dynamic response of the battery except at very low operating temperatures (e.g. -30 °C).

claims.claim: The fractional-order equivalent circuit model achieved an RMSE of 7.69 mV, with absolute error less than 20 mV and average relative error less than 0.1% on FUDS training data.
claims.quote: the calculated root-mean-square error (RMSE) is 7.69mV. Fig. 10 shows that the absolute error is basically in the range of 20mV... the average relative error is less than 0.1%

claims.claim: Under different operating conditions (HPPC, DST, FUDS), the RMSE values of the fractional-order model are all less than 10 mV.
claims.quote: Although the frequencies of the HPPC, DST, and FUDS operating currents are different, the RMSE values under different operating conditions are still less than 10mV

claims.claim: The fractional-order model RMSE at -20°C is 14.36 mV, compared to 6.45 mV at 25°C and 9.50 mV at 40°C, remaining below 15 mV across all tested temperatures.
claims.quote: RMSE values of models under different working conditions are still less than 15mV... Temperature: -20℃ RMSE (mV): 14.36; 25℃: 6.45; 40℃: 9.50

claims.claim: SOC estimation error based on the fractional model is within 0.02, while the error from the 2-RC model is within 0.07, under FUDS conditions with the extended Kalman filter.
claims.quote: the SOC estimation error based on the 2-RC model is within 0.07, while the error resulting from the fractional model is within 0.02.

claims.claim: The second-order RC model achieves a relative mean error of 0.396% and RMSE of 13.32 mV under FUDS conditions, compared to 0.085% and 7.54 mV for the fractional model under the same conditions.
claims.quote: Second-order RC model under FUDS condition: Relative Mean Error 0.396%, RMSE 13.32mV... Fractional model under FUDS condition: Relative Mean Error 0.085%, RMSE 7.54mv

claims.claim: The charging time in CC charging phase decreases with increasing battery degeneration degree, while the charging time in CV charging phase gradually increases as degeneration deepens.
claims.quote: the charging time in CC charging phase decreases with the degeneration degree, also pointed out that the performance of batteries can not be completely determined by CC charging process. Yang et al. [53], observed the current curve in CV charging phase of lithium batteries in different degeneration degrees, and concluded that as the degeneration degree of the battery is deepened, the changing rate of the current in the CV charging phase is gradually slowed down, which result in a gradual increase in the charging time in CV phase.

claims.claim: The shortening of CC charging time indicates deepening battery polarization, and increasing CV charging time indicates weakening of lithium intercalation ability.
claims.quote: The shortening of CC charging time indicates the deepening of battery polarization, and the increasing of CV charging time indicates the weakening of lithium intercalation ability [40].

claims.claim: Battery temperature in CC charging phase drops initially due to dominance of reversible endotherm from high initial reactant concentration, then rises due to irreversible exothermic heat, making CC phase mainly dominated by irreversible heat, while CV phase is co-dominant between reversible and irreversible heat.
claims.quote: the temperature drop in the beginning of the CC phase is obviously due to the dominance of reversible endotherm caused by high initial reactant concentration. Similarly, the temperature rise is caused by irreversible exothermic, and the temperature drop in the CV phase is the result of a combination of heat dissipation, electrochemical reaction endothermic and irreversible exothermic reduction. Therefore, the speculations should be corrected as follows: the CC charging phase is mainly dominated by irreversible heat, while CV charging phase is co-dominant.

claims.claim: Feature F5_tmp (temperature changing rate over equal charging voltage difference in CC phase) has a relatively low grey relational grade of 0.5675 compared to the other five features, indicating it cannot fully represent battery degeneration.
claims.quote: The relational grades of F5_tmp is relatively low compare to other 5 features, indicates that it can not fully represent battery degeneration.

claims.claim: A drastic change in ambient temperature within a single discharge-charge cycle causes failure of the extracted features to effectively characterize the battery system, resulting in catastrophic estimation performance in the proposed LSSVM-based SOH method.
claims.quote: As mentioned in Ref. [61], the sudden change of temperature will lead to a significant change in battery performance, which leads to the failure of the extracted features to effectively characterize the battery system. This explain the catastrophe point and also reveal the shortcoming of the proposed method, i.e, drastic changes of ambient temperature in a discharge-charge cycle will result in terrible estimation performance.

claims.claim: The proposed LSSVM-based SOH estimation method stabilizes SOH estimation errors below 0.02 after several cycles even when the initial F4(1) value is set incorrectly, demonstrating robustness.
claims.quote: even if the initial F4(1) leads to large errors, the proposed method can still stabilize the SOH estimation errors below 0.02 after several cycles, indicating the robustness of the proposed method.

claims.claim: LSSVM has training time complexity of O(n²) compared to O(n³) for standard SVM, but LSSVM typically has more support vectors than SVM resulting in higher estimation time complexity.
claims.quote: the training procedure of SVM has the time complexity about O(n3) [60], while is O(n2) for LSSVM (n is the number of training points). Both of their estimation complexity is O(m) (m is the number of SVs). Generally speaking, LSSVM has more SVs than SVM, so it costs more time to finish the estimation.

claims.claim: A fresh 21700 NCM/graphite cell (rated 3.6 V, 4 Ah) requires 6270 s to reach the pre-set cut-off voltage during CC charging, while the same cell at 80% SOH requires only approximately 4320 s, which is 68.9% of the fresh battery charging time.
claims.quote: The fresh battery takes 6270 s to reach its pre-set cut-off voltage, while the battery with 80% SOH merely requires about 4320 s, only 68.9% of that of fresh battery.

claims.claim: CC charging time is significantly shortened as the battery ages because polarization becomes more pronounced with the degradation of battery capacity.
claims.quote: It can be seen the CC charging time is significantly shortened as the battery ages, this is because the polarization becomes more obvious with the degradation of battery capacity.

claims.claim: A wider charging voltage range yields higher Pearson correlation with battery capacity but at the cost of more intensive computation.
claims.quote: the results clearly express that wider voltage range will bring higher correlation with the price of more intensive calculation.

claims.claim: The GA-optimized voltage range [3.467 V, 4.106 V] achieved ME of 1.11%, MAE of 0.18%, and RMSE of 0.23% for SOH estimation, which are approximately one-third and one-half respectively of those obtained with randomly selected parameters.
claims.quote: the GA can achieve the minimum ME, which is only one third of those by the randomly selected parameters. In addition, the MAE and RMSE by GA are both only half of those of the random selection approach of parameters.

claims.claim: Higher sampling frequency does not consistently lead to better SOH estimation performance; the ME at 1 s sampling time (1.37%) was larger than at 30 s sampling time (1.11%).
claims.quote: It can be intuitively found that higher sampling accuracy does not lead to better estimation performance all the time. The ME is 1.37% with the sampling time of 1 s; nevertheless, the ME is only 1.11% when the sampling time is 30 s.

claims.claim: The proposed fixed size LS-SVM achieved a maximum absolute error (ME) of 1.11%, which is approximately 58% of the ME of 1.93% produced by the traditional NN algorithm under the same feature inputs.
claims.quote: The ME, MAE and RMSE of the NN are respectively 1.93%, 0.22% and 0.29%; and nonetheless, the values by the fixed size LS-SVM are 1.11%, 0.18% and 0.23%. Particularly, the ME of proposed fixed size LS-SVM is only about 58% of that by the traditional NN algorithm.

claims.claim: When validated on three unfamiliar batteries, the SOH estimation error remained within 2% and the maximum RMSE and MAE were 0.44% and 0.34% respectively.
claims.quote: the estimation error, as depicted in Fig. 9 (b), (d) and (f), is all less within 2%. Table V summarizes the RMSE, ME and MAE of the SOH estimation results. The maximum RMSE and MAE in the three testing batteries are 0.44% and 0.34%, respectively.

claims.claim: For 18650-type NCM/graphite Li-ion batteries, the lower limit of the critical thermal runaway region decreases by about 40°C when SOC increases from 0% to 100%.
claims.quote: The lower limit of the critical region decreases by about 40 °C when the SOC increases from 0% to 100%.

claims.claim: The critical temperatures of thermal runaway predicted by the modified Thomas model are 212°C, 220°C, 179°C, 164°C, and 183°C for 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% SOC cells, respectively.
claims.quote: the critical temperatures of TR were predicted at 212 ℃, 220 ℃, 179 ℃, 164 ℃ and 183 ℃ for 0%, 25%, 50%, 75% and 100% SOC cells, respectively.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway at 205°C oven temperature, NCM cathode material decomposes to elemental nickel and oxides of nickel, cobalt, and manganese.
claims.quote: When the oven temperature reached 205 ℃, the XRD results exhibit the ternary NCM material decomposed to elemental nickel and oxides of nickel, cobalt, and manganese.

claims.claim: The open-circuit voltage of cells declines before the relief valve breaks during oven tests, which could serve as a safety warning indicator.
claims.quote: Comparing to the time of relief valve breaking, we find that the voltage of cells always declined ahead of relief valve breaking. This could be a useful information for the safety warning of the battery system.

claims.claim: The separator of the tested battery collapses at 184.3°C with only 7.26% shrinkage due to ceramic coating, and exhibits an exothermic peak of 137.6 mW/g at that temperature.
claims.quote: the separator collapses at 184.3 ℃ with only 7.26% of shrinkage due to the ceramic coating... The collapse of separator at 184 ℃ shown in Fig. 8f exhibits a 137.6 mW g-1 of an exothermic peak

claims.claim: The Biot number for the tested 18650 battery is 0.31, which exceeds 0.1, indicating that temperature distribution inside the battery cannot be neglected and the Thomas model is more appropriate than the Semenov model.
claims.quote: Bi = hL/k = 60 × 0.009 / 1.72 = 0.31 > 0.1. Thus, the temperature distribution inside the battery cannot be neglected, and the Thomas model is available for critical temperature calculation in this condition.

claims.claim: Model predictions for cells with 50% and 75% SOC are slightly lower than the critical regions measured by oven tests, attributed to the omission of separator melting and internal short-circuit effects in the thermal model.
claims.quote: the prediction for cells with 50% and 75% SOCs are slightly lower than the critical region measured by oven tests. The lack of considering separator melting and breaking in the thermal model could be the reason that causes the error.

claims.claim: At 185°C oven temperature, the regenerated SEI film on the anode surface becomes uneven with small particles, indicating that the reaction of embedded lithium intensifies at this temperature.
claims.quote: For the test at 185 ℃, the regenerated SEI film on anode surface became uneven accompanied by small particles, which indicates the reaction of embedded lithium intensified at this temperature.

claims.claim: When thermal runaway is triggered by uniform heating in NCM811 cells, the safety valve opens timely, only open flame occurs without explosion, no internal short circuit is caused, and the safety valve is not damaged.
claims.quote: When thermal runaway triggered by uniform heating, the safety valve is opened timely and only open flame occurred without explosion, it did not cause the internal short circuit, and the safety valve was not damaged.

claims.claim: When thermal runaway is triggered by local heating in NCM811 cells, a serious internal short circuit occurs, the released electrolyte and other material burn violently and explode, and the safety valve is completely damaged.
claims.quote: when thermal runaway triggered by the local heating method, it caused a serious internal short circuit, the released electrolyte and other material burned violently and exploded, and the safety valve was completely damaged.

claims.claim: Increasing nickel content in the positive electrode increases the degree of damage when thermal runaway is triggered by local heating, as shown by comparison of NCM111, NCM532, NCM622, and NCM811 batteries.
claims.quote: the results revealed that increase of nickel content in positive electrode would also increase the degree of damage when a TR triggered by local heating method.

claims.claim: The phase transition of charged NCM positive electrode materials begins at approximately 250°C, releasing active oxygen that reacts with the negative electrode and rapidly increases heat release.
claims.quote: the phase transition of the charged positive electrode materials begins at about 250°C and active oxygen is released, the oxygen reacts with the negative electrode, thus the heat release increases rapidly.

claims.claim: For NCM811 cells, thermal runaway can occur even without internal short circuit, even when the SEI film is completely removed and the electrolyte is completely drained, driven instead by oxygen release from positive electrode materials.
