claims.quote: The experimental results show that the TR occurs even if there is no internal short circuit, the SEI film is completely removed and the electrolyte is completely drained. When the positive and negative powders are mixed, there will be a sharp exothermic peak.

claims.claim: The critical temperature for thermal runaway onset was 101.5°C under uniform heating and 166.4°C under local heating for NCM811 cells at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: The results show that the critical temperature of TR was increased from 101.5°C in uniform heating mode to 166.4°C in local heating mode.

claims.claim: The rate of temperature rise during thermal runaway was 3.8°C/s under uniform heating and 44.2°C/s under local heating for NCM811 cells.
claims.quote: the rate of TR heating was 3.8°C/s and 44.2°C/s, respectively.

claims.claim: The maximum surface temperature after thermal runaway was 185.6°C under uniform heating and 388.2°C under local heating for NCM811 cells at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the surface maximum temperature after TR increased from 185.6°C in uniform heating mode to 388.2°C in local heating mode.

claims.claim: Mass loss during thermal runaway was 10.4% of total cell mass under uniform heating and 58.7% under local heating for NCM811 cells.
claims.quote: Mass Loss [was] 10.4% [for uniform heating] and 58.7% [for local heating].

claims.claim: The maximum negative electrode temperature difference of NCM811 cells during thermal runaway was higher than NCM111, NCM532, and NCM622 by 133°C, 107°C, and 84°C respectively.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature difference of the negative electrode is increased by 133°C, 107°C and, 84°C, respectively.

claims.claim: Before open fire during local heating-triggered thermal runaway, the maximum surface heat flux was 2005.28 W/m² with a rising rate of approximately 5 W/(m²·s), while internal heat accumulation was 47,430 J compared to only 2069 J dissipated through the surface.
claims.quote: the maximum value of q2-s was 2005.28 W/m2, and its rising rate was approximate 5 W/(m2,s), and then substituting q2-s into Eq. (1), the convective heat dissipation Q2-s on the surface of the cell is 2069J, and the internal heat accumulation in the cell Qinta is 47,430 J.

claims.claim: Under uniform heating, the internal structure of the NCM811 cell remained relatively intact after thermal runaway: the separator wrinkled but did not crack, copper and aluminum current collectors did not melt, and no large-scale internal short circuit occurred.
claims.quote: the carbon layer remained intact after TR triggered by uniform heating, the positive and negative electrode materials and metal current collectors-copper foil and aluminum foil were not melted, and the diaphragm wrinkled but not melted or broken.

claims.claim: Heat abuse is defined as heating the cell to above 101°C at a heating rate of more than 5°C/min from an external heat source, leading to thermal runaway reaction.
claims.quote: Heat abuse is generally defined as: when the external heat source heats the cell to above 101°C at a heating rate of more than 5°C/min, which leads to TR reaction.

claims.claim: The triggering temperature of thermal runaway decreases with increasing Ni content in NCM cathode materials, while total heat production increases with Ni content.
claims.quote: During the TR process, the triggering temperature of the cell decreased with the increase of Ni content, while the total heat production also increased.

claims.claim: At 50°C, Mn begins to dissolve into the electrolyte in NCM cathode materials, which is identified as a side reaction of high-temperature energy attenuation.
claims.quote: When the temperature reaches 50°C, Mn begins to dissolve into the electrolyte, which is a side reaction of the high temperature energy attenuation.

claims.claim: The driving range of EVs at 23.9°C is 105 miles, which reduces by 59% to 43 miles at -6.7°C.
claims.quote: the driving range of EVs is 105 miles at 23.9 (cid:1)C, as reported by the American Automobile Association, which reduces by 59% to 43 miles at (cid:3)6.7 (cid:1)C

claims.claim: At -20°C, battery voltage reduced to below the battery management limit and normal discharge operation could not be performed.
claims.quote: At (cid:3)20 (cid:1)C, the voltage reduced to below the battery management limit, and normal discharge operation could not be performed.

claims.claim: Discharge capacities at 2.0 A for a 18650-type LiCoO2/graphite battery are 98.1%, 86.1%, 82.6%, 78.3%, 73.7%, and 68.5% of nominal capacity at 22°C, 0°C, -5°C, -10°C, -15°C, and -20°C, respectively.
claims.quote: the discharge capacities corresponding to 22 (cid:1)C, 0 (cid:1)C, (cid:3)5 (cid:1)C, (cid:3)10 (cid:1)C,-15 (cid:1)C, and (cid:3)20 (cid:1)C are calibrated to 98.1%, 86.1%, 82.6%, 78.3%, 73.7%, and 68.5%, respectively, under a 2.0 A current

claims.claim: Discharge capacities at 3.0 A for a 18650-type LiCoO2/graphite battery are 97.6%, 83.7%, 79.3%, 73.1%, 65.7%, and 55.9% of nominal capacity at 22°C, 0°C, -5°C, -10°C, -15°C, and -20°C, respectively.
claims.quote: 97.6%, 83.7%, 79.3%, 73.1%, 65.7%, and 55.9%, respectively, under a 3.0 A current

claims.claim: The initial voltage loss at -20°C is approximately seven times higher than that at 22°C, relative to the fully charged cutoff voltage of 4.2 V.
claims.quote: Corresponding to the completely charged cutoff voltage of 4.2 V, the initial voltage loss at (cid:3)20 (cid:1)C is approximately seven times higher than that at 22 (cid:1)C.

claims.claim: Ohmic resistance values at DOD of 0.5 under 2.0 A discharge are 46.4 mΩ, 66.1 mΩ, 75.5 mΩ, 86.5 mΩ, 103.9 mΩ, and 124.9 mΩ at 22°C, 5°C, 0°C, -5°C, -10°C, and -15°C, respectively.
claims.quote: the ohmic resistance values at a DOD of 0.5 are 46.4 mU, 66.1 mU, 75.5 mU, 86.5 mU, 103.9 mU, and 124.9 mU at 22 (cid:1)C, 5 (cid:1)C, 0 (cid:1)C, (cid:3)5 (cid:1)C, (cid:3)10 (cid:1)C, and (cid:3)15 (cid:1)C under a current of 2.0 A, respectively

claims.claim: Ohmic resistance remains essentially unchanged versus DOD in the range of 0.2 to 0.7, but is larger and dependent on both temperature and DOD when DOD is less than 0.2 or greater than 0.7.
claims.quote: the ohmic resistance remains essentially unchanged versus DOD but is affected by temperature from a DOD of 0.2e0.7. However, the ohmic resistance is both dependent on operation temperature and DOD when the DOD is less than 0.2 and when it exceeds 0.7, and the value is larger than that in the DOD range of 0.2e0.7.

claims.claim: At constant ambient temperature, ohmic resistance decreases as discharge current increases, and a higher discharge current leads to a smaller change in amplitude of ohmic resistance for the same temperature difference.
claims.quote: when the ambient temperature remains constant, the ohmic resistance decreases as the discharge current increases. For the same ambient temperature difference, a higher discharge current leads to a smaller change in amplitude of the ohmic resistance.

claims.claim: At -20°C compared to -10°C at DOD of 0.5, ohmic resistance increases by 107.9 mΩ, 38.5 mΩ, and 19.3 mΩ under discharge currents of 1 A, 2 A, and 3 A, respectively.
claims.quote: compared with the value of ohmic resistance at (cid:3)10 (cid:1)C, the ohmic resistance values at (cid:3)20 (cid:1)C at a DOD of 0.5 increase by 107.9 mU, 38.5 mU, and 19.3 mU under discharge currents of 1 A, 2 A, and 3 A, respectively.

claims.claim: Polarization resistance not only increases as temperature decreases but also gradually increases as discharge continues, and dramatically increases at the end of discharge.
claims.quote: the polarization resistance not only increases as the temperature decreases but also gradually increases as the discharge continues, and dramatically increases at the end of discharge.

claims.claim: During normal temperature operation, ohmic resistance dominates the discharge process, whereas polarization resistance plays a pivotal role at low temperature.
claims.quote: During normal temperature operation, the ohmic resistance is in a dominant status throughout the discharge process, however, the polarization resistance acts a pivotal role at low temperature.

claims.claim: The maximum ratio of polarization resistance to ohmic resistance is 1.43 and 1.39 under discharge currents of 2 A and 3 A, respectively.
claims.quote: when the battery discharges under current values of 2 A and 3 A, the maximum ratio of polarization resistance to ohmic resistance is 1.43 and 1.39, respectively.

claims.claim: Heat generation values at 0.5 DOD at -10°C are 0.28 W, 0.73 W, 1.31 W, and 2.41 W for discharge currents of 1 A, 2 A, 3 A, and 6 A, respectively.
claims.quote: the heat generation values at 0.5 DOD are 0.28 W, 0.73 W, 1.31 W, and 2.41 W corresponding to discharge currents of 1 A, 2 A, 3 A, and 6 A, respectively, at (cid:3)10 (cid:1)C.

claims.claim: Irreversible heat has a quadratic relation with current, while reversible heat is linearly proportional to current.
claims.quote: irreversible heat has a quadratic relation with current, and the reversible heat is linearly proportional to current, indicating that the total heat generation is considerably affected by the operation current.

claims.claim: The ratio of reversible to irreversible heat generation under 2 A discharge increases from 9.6% at -20°C to 24.4% at 22°C.
claims.quote: as the battery discharges under a 2 A current, the ratios become 9.6%, 11.7%, 12.3%, 14.1%, 15.7%, and 24.4% at (cid:3)20 (cid:1)C, (cid:3)15 (cid:1)C, (cid:3)10 (cid:1)C, (cid:3)5 (cid:1)C, 0 (cid:1)C, and 22 (cid:1)C, respectively.

claims.claim: The ratio of reversible to irreversible heat generation at -10°C is 16.7%, 12.3%, 10.1%, and 9.7% for currents of 1 A, 2 A, 3 A, and 6 A, respectively.
claims.quote: the ratios are 16.7%, 12.3%, 10.1%, and 9.7% corresponding to currents of 1 A, 2 A, 3 A, and 6 A at (cid:3)10 (cid:1)C ambient temperature.

claims.claim: Reversible heat cannot be neglected when the battery is discharged under a small current and relatively high ambient temperature, especially at the end stage of discharge where the high entropic heat coefficient leads to a sizable quantity of reversible heat.
claims.quote: reversible heat is not a negligible quantity when battery is discharged under a small current and under a relatively high ambient temperature; especially at the end stage of discharge, the high entropic heat coefﬁcient leads to a sizable quantity of reversible heat. Neglecting this reversible heat will result in a large deviation of the model calculation results.

claims.claim: When DOD exceeds 0.8, the entropic heat coefficient becomes negative, indicating an exothermic state.
claims.quote: When the DOD is greater than 0.8, the entropic heat coefﬁcient becomes negative, indicating an exothermic state.

claims.claim: The entropic heat coefficient is not significantly affected by low temperature operation and is almost only dependent on state of charge, unlike battery aging which causes distinct changes in entropic heat coefficient.
claims.quote: There are little deviations between the entropic heat coefﬁcients measured under different currents... which indicates that normal operation at low temperature does not cause irreversible damage to the battery structure and performance. This mechanism is different from the effect of battery aging on the entropic heat coefﬁcient.

claims.claim: The Biot number for the 18650-type battery under the tested conditions is 0.037, which is less than 0.1, validating use of the lumped-parameter model.
claims.quote: In this work, h is 26.57 W m(cid:3)2 K(cid:3)1. Consequently, the Biot number becomes 0.037, which is less than 0.1. The lumped-parameter model can be employed for the calculation.

claims.claim: Low-temperature IC curves show the same appearances as aged battery: reduced peak number, weakened peak value, and peak shift to low-voltage plateau, indicating low temperature has similar effects on battery as aging at normal temperature.
claims.quote: low-temperature IC curves show the same appearances as the aged battery, the peak number is reduced, the peak value is weakened, and the peak is shifted to the low-voltage plateau. It revealed that the impact of low temperature on the battery is similar to battery aging at normal temperature.

claims.claim: Low-temperature operation can lead to deposition and precipitation of lithium ions on the electrode surface, which can puncture the SEI film, causing a short circuit and risk of battery fire or explosion.
claims.quote: low-temperature operation will lead to the deposition and precipitation of lithium ions on the electrode surface; these deposited lithium ions can puncture the SEI ﬁlm, causing a short circuit and the risk of battery ﬁre or even explosion

claims.claim: The maximum temperature deviation between the lumped-parameter model and measured values is 2.3°C, occurring at the end of 6 A discharge.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature deviation is 2.3 (cid:1)C, which occurs at the end of 6 A discharge.

claims.claim: TR induced by overcharging of 0.5C and 1.0C produces peak heat release rates of 60.8 kW and 70.4 kW respectively, which are much higher than those of 11.5 kW and 5.7 kW induced by overheating of 300 W and 400 W.
claims.quote: the peak heat release rate of cell 1#'s TR induced by overcharging of 0.5C and 1.0C reach 60.8 kW and 70.4 kW, respectively, which are much higher than those of 11.5 kW and 5.7 kW induced by overheating of 300 W and 400 W.

claims.claim: The total heat release of TR induced by overcharging of 0.5C and 1.0C is almost 4 to 6 times that induced by overheating of 300 W and 400 W for the 38 Ah NCM hard-shell battery tested.
claims.quote: the total heat release of cell 1#'s TR induced by overcharging of 0.5C and 1.0C is almost 4 to 6 times that induced by overheating of 300 W and 400 W.

claims.claim: The critical thermal energy (Qheater) required to trigger TR in fully charged 38 Ah NCM batteries may be constant under overheating powers of 300 W and 400 W, with values of 131.30 kJ and 132.95 kJ respectively, and heating power only affects the time required to trigger TR.
claims.quote: the critical Qheater is not much different under the overheating of 300 W and 400 W. It can be inferred the critical Qheater may be a constant with a certain error under the overheating of 300 W and 400 W. The heating power only affect the time (t1#,tr) required for the battery to be triggered to TR.

claims.claim: Chemical and joule heat contribution (Qchem&jou;1#) at the critical TR state increases with increasing heating power, rising from 37.44 kJ at 300 W to 45.70 kJ at 400 W, due to the low thermal conductivity in the thickness direction of the battery (1.5 W/m K).
claims.quote: Noting that the value of Qchem&jou;1# shows a certain increase with increasing the heating power. On the one hand, this is because the maximum temperature the heating plate can reach increases with increasing the heating power, and the maximum temperature directly affects the rate of the side reaction inside the battery.

claims.claim: In an open environment, TR propagation induced by overheating of 300 W and 400 W is faster than that induced by overcharging of 0.5C and 1.0C, because the back surface temperature of cell 1# under overheating is higher, resulting in greater heat transfer power through the shell to cell 2#.
claims.quote: the Dt1#-2# of 300 W and 400 W are shorter than those of 0.5C and 1.0C, while the Ph;ave of 300 W and 400 W are larger than those of 0.5C and 1.0C...the T1-b of cell 1# under overheating of 300 W and 400 W is higher than that of cell 1# under overcharging of 0.5C and 1.0C, thus the heat transfer power of cell 1# to cell 2# through the shell under overheating of 300 W and 400 W is higher than that under the overcharging of 0.5C and 1.0C.

claims.claim: The critical electric energy triggering TR (Qe;1#) and critical self-generated heat (Qge;1#) slightly decrease with increasing overcharging rate, with Qe;1# values of 516.88 kJ at 0.5C and 511.50 kJ at 1.0C, and the ratio Qge;1#/Qe;1# remains approximately constant at ~22% regardless of overcharging rate.
claims.quote: the values of DQin;1#, Qge;1#, Qe;1# and Qtrans;1-2# of cell 1# under 1.0C rate are slightly lower than those of cell 1# under 0.5C. Noting the ration of Qge;1# and Qe;1# for 1C and 0.5C is almost the same, indicating the ration is slightly affected by the overcharging rate.

claims.claim: Mass loss induced by overcharging at 0.5C and 1.0C is almost twice the mass loss induced by overheating at 300 W and 400 W for the 38 Ah NCM battery, and greater mass loss under overcharging causes cell 1# to become more hollow, reducing contact stress and heat transfer power to cell 2# compared to overheating conditions.
claims.quote: the mass loss induced by overcharging rate of 0.5C and 1.0C is almost twice the mass loss induced by 300 W and 400 W heating...because the mass loss of cell 1# under overcharging is larger than that of cell 1# under overheating, cell 1# under overcharge is more empty than that under overheating, suggesting the contact stress between cell 1# and cell 2# under overheating is greater that under overcharging, which in turn leads to the heat transfer power between cell 1# and cell 2# under overheating through the shell is larger than that under overcharging.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rate of TR induced by overheating of 300 W and 400 W is more than twice that of TR induced by overcharging of 0.5C and 1.0C for the 38 Ah NCM hard-shell battery.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature rate of TR induced by overheating of 300 W and 400 W is more than twice that of TR induced by overcharging of 0.5C and 1.0C.

claims.claim: Internal short circuit (ISC) in a 24Ah Li(NiCoMn)1/3O2 pouch cell during thermal abuse was caused by thermal shrinkage of the polyethylene separator due to PE melting, which occurs when battery temperature exceeds approximately 130°C.
claims.quote: The shrinkage of the separator was mainly caused by PE melting, which happened when the battery temperature exceeded around 130℃. Though ceramic coating can help to lower the shrinkage rate, the separator still kept shrinking with the increase of temperature, leading to increased risk of ISC.

claims.claim: Battery resistance increased approximately 1000 times (from 1.5 mΩ to a peak of 1.808 Ω) before ISC occurred during thermal abuse of the 24Ah Li(NiCoMn)1/3O2 cell, which limited the short circuit current and thus generated little joule heat.
claims.quote: The battery resistance was 1.5mΩ at the beginning of TR process and increased to 0.117Ω at around 130℃... the battery resistance increased rapidly and peaked at 1.808Ω, 1000 times larger than the initial value... The extremely large battery resistance limited the short circuit current IISC and the consequent joule heat to a small value during ISC, and thus no visible increase was observed in the temperature rate profile.

claims.claim: In the 24Ah Li(NiCoMn)1/3O2 battery, exothermic reactions between anode and electrolyte (An-Ele system) were identified as the trigger of thermal runaway, with T2 of 243.5°C, in contrast to the Ca-An partial cell which did not enter TR until 314.7°C.
claims.quote: the An-Ele partial cell had a T2 of 243.5℃, close to that of full cell, while the Ca-An partial cell did not go into TR until 314.7℃. Therefore, we can confirm that the reactions between anode and electrolyte were responsible for triggering TR inside the battery.

claims.claim: The Ca-Ele partial cell did not undergo thermal runaway (maximum temperature rate 0.186°C·s⁻¹, below the 1°C·s⁻¹ criterion) because cathode solvents vaporize before oxygen is released from Li(NiCoMn)1/3O2 cathode at around 250°C.
claims.quote: the Ca-Ele partial cell had a T3 of 355.3℃ and maximum temperature rate of 0.186℃∙s-1, less than the criterion of TR (1℃∙s-1)... due to the vaporization of the solvents (the boiling temperatures of DMC, EMC and EC are 91 °C, 110 °C and 248 °C, respectively) before the release of oxygen from the Li(NiCoMn)1/3O2 cathode at around 250 °C.

claims.claim: For a 21Ah lithium-ion battery with a bare PE separator (without ceramic coating), ISC occurred at 124.0°C when PE shrinkage started and, because battery resistance was still low, generated sufficient joule heat to trigger local thermal runaway directly.
claims.quote: The 21Ah lithium-ion battery had a bare PE separator with poor thermal stability. As shown in Fig. 11 (b), ISC occurred at 124.0℃ when shrinkage of PE started. The battery had a low resistance at 124.0℃, as the majority of the electrolyte remained inside. Therefore, ISC generated a large amount of joule heat, leading to local TR in the ISC area and further TR propagation among the whole battery.

claims.claim: Simulation results show that for batteries with resistance equal to or 5 times the initial value (R0 = 1.5 mΩ), ISC produces a local temperature rise greater than 100°C sufficient to trigger local thermal runaway, while at 100 or 1000 times the initial resistance, the maximum local temperature rise decreases to less than 10°C.
claims.quote: For the batteries with a small resistance (R=R0,5R0), the maximum temperature rise is larger than 100℃ and is enough to trigger local TR. For the batteries with a resistance of 10 or 20R0, the temperature rise is limited in the ISC area. For the batteries with a large resistance (100R0, 1000R0), the maximum local temperature rise decreases to less than 10℃.

claims.claim: After liquid-nitrogen-ceased thermal runaway at TISC, the NCM/Gr coin cell retained more than 90% of initial discharge capacity and total capacity, demonstrating that most chemical energy remained stored in the battery rather than being released during ISC.
claims.quote: The initial discharge capacity and total capacity of the LN-cooled NCM/Gr coin cell remained more than 90% of those of pristine NCM/Gr coin cell. That demonstrates that most of the chemical energy was stored inside the battery rather than released during ISC, consistent with the changes in temperature rate profiles.

claims.claim: The sharp increase of battery resistance before ISC results from three concurrent mechanisms: depletion of electrolyte due to solvent evaporation, porosity reduction and thick deposit formation on electrode surfaces, and shutdown of the separator due to PE pore closure.
claims.quote: the battery suffered from significant impedance increase during TR process, resulting from the following reasons: 1) severe depletion of electrolyte due to solvents evaporation and side reactions on both cathode and anode surface; 2) formation of thick deposits and porosity reduction in both cathode and anode; 3) shutdown of separator.

claims.claim: In the Coulomb counting method for SOC estimation, accumulation of start-up and current sensor errors results in drift and poor accuracy.
claims.quote: the accumulation of the start-up and current sensor errors results into a drift and poor accuracy

claims.claim: The OCV-SOC correlation for SOC estimation holds only when the battery reaches an equilibrium state, defined as no operation for several minutes or hours.
claims.quote: this correlation holds only when the battery gets to an equilibrium state (i.e., no operation for several minutes or hours)

claims.claim: Temperature variation introduces an offset in both the Vb-SOC and OCV-SOC curves as well as in the equivalent series resistance Rbat.
claims.quote: temperature variation introduces an offset in both Vb-SOC and OCV-SOC curves as well as in the equivalent series resistance Rbat

claims.claim: The battery's EOL equivalent series resistance REOL is defined as 160% of the brand new battery's equivalent series resistance Rnew.
claims.quote: a battery's EOL equivalent series resistance REOL is taken as 160% brand new battery's equivalent series resistance Rnew (i.e., REOL = Rnew × 160%)

claims.claim: Ohmic and polarization resistances of lithium-ion batteries increase significantly as temperature decreases.
claims.quote: results report a significant increase in ohmic and polarization resistances as temperature decreases

claims.claim: Low battery resistance values combined with low sensor precision and measurement noise make accurate SOH estimation difficult, and many estimation procedures fail to predict SOH with high accuracy.
claims.quote: low battery's resistance values combined with low sensors' precision and measurements' noise make accurate estimation a difficult task to undertake and many estimation procedures fail to predict SOH with high accuracy

claims.claim: The SOC reference definition using the OCV-SOC curve is set at a temperature of 25°C, as specified by the battery manufacturer.
claims.quote: As indicated by the battery's manufacturer, The SOC is defined using the OCV-SOC curve at a temperature of 25°C

claims.claim: When 5% graphite oxide was added to facilitate exfoliation of kaolinite by high-shear mixer, 95% of obtained nanosheets were distributed between 0.1-0.7 μm with fewer than 5 layers, compared to 74% in the 0.1-0.7 μm range for directly exfoliated kaolinite without graphite oxide.
claims.quote: when 5% graphite oxide was added to facilitate exfoliation, 95% of obtained nanosheets were distributed between 0.1-0.7 μm, in which the number of layers was less than 5 layers... the lateral size distribution between 0.1-0.7 μm is 74% for 0-G/K/P and 95% for 5-G/K/P

claims.claim: The BET specific surface area of graphite oxide-assisted exfoliated kaolinite (5-G/K/P, 21.47 m²/g) is 2.1 times that of directly exfoliated kaolinite (0-G/K/P, 10.38 m²/g), which is attributed to exposure of more surfaces from layer exfoliation and formation of nanoscrolls structure.
claims.quote: The specific surface area of 0-G/K/P and 5-G/K/P is 10.38 and 21.47 m2/g, respectively. The specific surface area of 5-G/K/P is significantly higher than that of 0-G/K/P, which is attributed to the exfoliation of kaolinite layers exposing more surfaces and the formation of nanoscrolls structure.

claims.claim: The theoretical maximum MB adsorption capacity of graphite oxide-assisted exfoliated kaolinite (5-G/K/P) is 250 mg/g, compared to 111 mg/g for directly exfoliated kaolinite (0-G/K/P), as determined by the Langmuir isotherm model.
claims.quote: The Qm of 0-G/K/P and 5-G/K/P are 111 and 250 mg/g, which indicates that the adsorption performance of MB is improved obviously by the auxiliary exfoliation of graphite oxide.

claims.claim: At 4000 rpm, kaolinite nanoscrolls have a length of 0.2-0.9 μm with ~90% between 0.3-0.7 μm (average 0.49 μm), but when rotational speed increases to 6000 rpm, average nanoscroll length decreases to ~0.25 μm with ~73% between 0.2-0.3 μm, because higher shear creates smaller particles that are difficult to curl into nanoscrolls.
claims.quote: At the rotation speed of 4000 rpm, the nanoscrolls have a length of 0.2-0.9 μm, in which about 90% are between 0.3 to 0.7 μm, and the average length is 0.49 μm... when the rotation speed increases to 6000 rpm, the average length of the nanoscrolls is about 0.25 μm, in which about 73% are between 0.2 to 0.3 μm. The reason is that with the increase of rotational speed, the size of the exfoliated kaolinite particles becomes smaller, making it difficult to curl into nanoscrolls structure.

claims.claim: The adsorption capacity of a physical mixture of 5% GO and 95% directly exfoliated kaolinite is 166.65 mg/g, significantly lower than that of 5-G/K/P (250 mg/g), indicating that the enhanced adsorption of 5-G/K/P is not a simple additive mixture effect of GO and kaolinite.
claims.quote: The adsorption capacity of 5% GO and 95% directly exfoliated kaolinite is 166.65 mg/g, significantly lower than that of 5-G/K/P (250 mg/g), indicating that the adsorption capacity of 5-G/K/P to MB is not a simple mixed by GO and kaolinite.

claims.claim: For 22 Ah LiFePO4/graphite batteries, the average trigger temperature of thermal runaway is 198.6°C for 50% SOC and 184.8°C for 100% SOC, with higher SOC resulting in lower onset temperature of TR.
claims.quote: The average trigger temperatures of TR are 198.6 and 184.8 °C for 50 and 100% SOC batteries, respectively. With an increase in the SOCs, the onset time and temperature of TR decrease.

claims.claim: No thermal runaway occurs in 0% SOC LiFePO4/graphite batteries under overheating abuse conditions.
claims.quote: No TR occurs in 0% SOC battery.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC LiFePO4/graphite batteries, jet fire is blown out by a powerful elliptical column of gas flow, resulting in incomplete combustion, whereas jet fire is observed but not extinguished for 50% SOC batteries.
claims.quote: For the case of the 100% SOC battery, the phenomenon (jet ﬂow) was more violent: the ﬂame near the cathode tab was suddenly blown out by the powerful elliptical column of gas ﬂow.

claims.claim: The peak HRR of 100% SOC cells (6.15 kW) is smaller than that of 50% SOC cells (12.09 kW) because the jet fire of 100% SOC cells burned incompletely due to flame extinguishment, while the highest peak HRR of 13.91 kW is observed for 75% SOC cells.
claims.quote: the peak value of 100% SOC is smaller than that of 50% SOC batteries...This was because the jet ﬁre of 100% SOC cells burnt incompletely...the peak HRRs as high as 13.91 kW are observed for 75% SOC cells.

claims.claim: CO production and HF production both increase with increasing SOC in LiFePO4/graphite batteries, with the nCO2/nCO molar ratio decreasing from 32.15 at 0% SOC to 23.44 at 50% SOC to 9.26 at 100% SOC, indicating lower combustion efficiency at higher SOC.
claims.quote: it is found that CO production increases with the increasing SOCs. The nCO2/ nCO molar ratio can reﬂect the combustion situation, which reached approximately 32.15, 23.44 and 9.26 for 0%, 50% and 100% SOC cells, respectively.

claims.claim: H2 is only detected for 100% SOC LiFePO4/graphite batteries during combustion; no H2 is detected for 0% and 50% SOC cases because H2 is burned in those cases.
claims.quote: Unlike the 100% SOC cells, no H2 was detected for 0 and 50% SOC cases due to the H2 being burnt.

claims.claim: The overall mass loss during burning increases with SOC: 98.6 g (16.17%) for 0% SOC, 111.8 g (18.31%) for 50% SOC, and 135.7 g (22.26%) for 100% SOC batteries.
claims.quote: The overall mass loss (mass loss ratio) during the burning process is 98.6 g (16.17%), 111.8 g (18.31%), 135.7 g (22.26%) for 0%,50% and 100%SOC battery, respectively.

claims.claim: The maximum surface temperature and maximum temperature rate have no direct relation with HRR during the burning process of LiFePO4 batteries.
claims.quote: It should be noted that the maximum surface temperature of battery surface has no direct relation with HRR during the burning process.

claims.claim: Assuming HF production from a 100% SOC LiFePO4 battery fire is concentrated in a room of 20 m3, the HF concentration would be 0.13 g/m3, which far exceeds the IDLH value of 0.025 g/m3.
claims.quote: assuming that the ﬁre is under-ventilated and the HF production is concentrated in a room of 20 m3, the HF concentration is 0.13 g/m3, which far exceeds the Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health (IDLH) value of 0.025 g/m3.

claims.claim: The average temperature at voltage drop (indicating separator melting and internal short circuit onset) is 162.3–173.2°C for LiFePO4/graphite batteries, which is higher than the melting point of PE (135°C) and PP (165°C) separators.
claims.quote: the average temperature of voltage drop is 162.3~173.2 °C, which is higher than the melting point of the PE(135 °C) and PP (165 °C).

claims.claim: After separator melting causes internal short circuit, there is no immediate thermal runaway, providing an early warning interval of 186–199 s from safety valve venting to TR.
claims.quote: After the voltage falls, there is no immediate TR, which can provide an early warning for TR...It takes 186–199 s from the safety valve venting to TR, which can provide a possible early warning.

claims.claim: The delithiated Li0FePO4 decomposes and releases O2 at approximately 180°C, and the released oxygen can react with active battery materials to intensify exothermic reactions and trigger thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The delithiated Li0FePO4 decomposes through 2Li0FePO4 → Fe2P2O7 + 0.5O2 and releases O2 at the temperature of 180 °C. The released oxygen can react with many active materials used in the battery.

claims.claim: Battery components are stable at temperatures below 40°C, but above 40°C the battery is susceptible to events that destroy its reversible capabilities, such as capacity fade.
claims.quote: These components are stable at low temperatures < 40 °C. However, above 40 °C, the battery is susceptible to events that destroy its reversible capabilities, such as capacity fade.

claims.claim: The SEI layer is the least thermally stable portion of the unit cell and exothermically decomposes first at around 110°C, with the fastest kinetics among unit cell components.
claims.quote: The SEI layer is the least thermally stable portion of the unit cell and thus exothermically decomposes first, at around 110 °C, and has the fastest kinetics.

claims.claim: Initial SEI decomposition occurs at 80–120°C with a peak at around 100°C according to Feng et al.
claims.quote: A recent study by Feng et al. [16] indicates that this initial SEI decomposition occurs at 80–120 °C, with the peak at located around 100 °C.

claims.claim: The heat released during SEI decomposition scales with the amount of exposed surface area of the electrode, and higher surface area anodes have a higher enthalpy of reaction than anodes with lower surface area.
claims.quote: The heat released scales with the amount of exposed surface area [14,17–19]; higher surface area anodes have a higher enthalpy of reaction than anodes with a lower surface area.

claims.claim: At higher states of charge, more lithium is intercalated in the electrode and thus there is a potential for larger amounts of energy to be released during the lithium consumption reaction.
claims.quote: At higher states of charge, more lithium is intercalated in the electrode and thus, there is a potential for larger amounts of energy to be released.

claims.claim: LiFePO4 iron phosphate structure can remain stable in some cases until temperatures higher than 250°C and does not release oxygen, unlike cobalt and manganese chemistries.
claims.quote: LiFePO4 consists of an iron phosphate structure that can remain stable, in some cases, until temperatures higher than 250 °C. The iron phosphate structure does not alter structurally at the majority of temperatures studied in the literature thus, no changes in heat capacity and no oxygen release is expected, as is the case with both cobalt and manganese chemistries.

claims.claim: Balanced reactions between SEI decomposition and regeneration break at temperatures of 250°C or higher, leading to lithium deintercalation.
claims.quote: The recent study by Feng et al. [16] indicates that the balanced reactions between SEI decomposition and regeneration will break at temperatures of 250 °C or higher, leading to lithium deintercalation.

claims.claim: 12% of the total heat released during thermal runaway by a battery is enough to trigger thermal runaway in an adjacent battery.
claims.quote: The tests revealed that 12% of the total heat released during thermal runaway by a battery is enough to trigger thermal runaway in the adjacent battery.

claims.claim: Temperature differences as high as approximately 800°C were observed within a battery during nail penetration induced thermal runaway tests.
claims.quote: Temperature difference as high as ~800 °C was observed within the battery during the nail penetration induced thermal runaway tests.

claims.claim: A minichannel cooling system cannot prevent thermal runaway in a battery cell even at high coolant flow rates of approximately 10 L/min, but if coolant flow rate can be independently controlled for individual cells, the system can prevent propagation to neighboring cells.
claims.quote: The results showed that the minichannel cooling system cannot prevent thermal runaway in a battery cell even at high coolant flow rates of ~10 L min-1. However, if the coolant flow rate can be independently controlled for individual cells, the minichannel system can prevent the propagation of thermal runaway to neighboring cells.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway triggered by a short circuit in a prismatic battery expands initially lengthways along the electrodes and then radially until reaching the cell wall, after which it rapidly propagates along the length of the cell.
claims.quote: A short circuit produces thermal runaway reactions that expand initially lengthways along the electrodes and then radially until reaching the wall of the cell; once thermal runaway has reached the cell edges, it rapidly propagates along the length of the cell.

claims.claim: Using a reduced accuracy lumped model underpredicts the time to reach thermal runaway as well as the average and maximum temperatures reached inside the battery.
claims.quote: Using a reduced accuracy lumped model under predicts the time to reach thermal runaway as well as the average and maximum temperatures reached inside the battery.

claims.claim: Inaccurate battery material properties play only a small part in predicted thermal runaway behavior, while initial temperature, applied heat transfer coefficient, and electrode void fraction have a large effect.
claims.quote: Inaccurate battery material properties are shown to play only a small part in the predicted thermal runaway behavior while initial temperature of simulation, applied heat transfer coefficient and electrode void fraction have a large effect on the thermal runaway response.

claims.claim: The bulk model predicts a peak total cell temperature 31% higher than the segregated model, occurring in approximately half the duration of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Peak total cell temperatures of 309 °C and 236 °C, Fig. 9b, are reached for the bulk and segregated models, respectively... Thus, the bulk model predicts a temperature 31% higher than the segregated model, which occurs during half the duration of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: When thermal runaway reaches 10 mm along the battery, the temperature in adjacent layers has risen sufficiently to cause thermal runaway in adjacent unit cells, after which thermal runaway spreads radially and then rapidly along the battery length at a rate of 4 mm/s.
claims.quote: When the thermal runaway condition reaches 10 mm along the battery, the temperature in adjacent layers has risen sufficiently to cause thermal runaway in these adjacent unit cells. Thermal runaway then spreads outwards in the radial direction until it reaches the battery edges. The low heat transfer coefficient prevents rapid removal of the heat generated and at this point thermal runaway spreads rapidly along the length of the battery at a rate of 4 mm s-1.

claims.claim: A short circuit of 110 W causes partial decomposition of negative and positive electrodes, while a 220 W short circuit causes complete thermal runaway in the battery in a much shorter duration.
claims.quote: A short circuit of 110 W causes partial decomposition reactions of the negative and positive electrodes. A 220 W short circuit causes complete thermal runaway in the battery in a much shorter duration.

claims.claim: Low surface area negative electrodes have more lithium intercalated per unit mass, making them less stable at elevated temperatures, and a low surface area electrode breaks down more easily than a high surface area electrode and releases more heat during a short circuit.
claims.quote: In contrast, the low surface area electrodes have more lithium intercalated per unit mass, making them less stable at elevated temperatures... Thus, a low surface area negative electrode breaks down easier than a high surface area negative electrode and releases more heat during a short circuit.

claims.claim: Increasing the electrode void fraction from 0.70 to 0.85 results in complete positive electrode decomposition and complete breakdown of the negative electrode within 350 s, whereas a void fraction of 0.55 results in only complete positive electrode decomposition with no noticeable negative electrode decomposition after the short circuit ends.
claims.quote: A void fraction of 0.85 results in the entire positive electrode decomposing and complete breakdown of the negative electrode within 350 s. This compares with only 85% of the negative electrode breaking down with a void fraction of 0.70. In contrast, a void fraction of 0.55 results in complete positive electrode decomposition; however, due to the lower density of lithium in this cell, not enough heat is released per unit volume to produce a noticeable decomposition of the negative electrode once the short circuit has ended.

claims.claim: In a LiFePO4 battery, low thermal conductivity electrodes partially contain thermal runaway locally and the battery does not produce local uncontrolled temperature peaks as seen with LiCoO2, but instead produces a slow overall heating of triggered regions.
claims.quote: counterintuitively, low thermal conductivity electrodes partially contain thermal runaway locally. Furthermore, thermal runaway within a LiFePO4 battery does not produce local uncontrolled peaks in temperature within the cell that are seen with LiCoO2; rather it produces a slow overall heating of the triggered regions.

claims.claim: From a thermal management perspective, external sensors used to detect elevated internal temperatures would likely be ineffective in predicting the presence of thermal runaway in a LiFePO4 battery until the reactions have progressed irreversibly.
claims.quote: From a thermal management perspective, external sensors used to detect the elevated internal temperatures would likely be ineffective in predicting the presence of thermal runaway until the reactions have progressed irreversibly.

claims.claim: Increasing the heat transfer coefficient from 3.7 to 300 W/m²K on a single cell can mitigate complete cell failure, but obtaining this coefficient on all vertical edges of a pouch cell in a tightly packed battery pack is described as almost impossible.
claims.quote: Thus, increasing the heat removal rate, as applied to a single cell, could mitigate complete cell failure. However, cells are usually contained in a tightly packed pouch with air as the working fluid passing between pouches. This configuration makes obtaining a heat transfer coefficient of 300 W m-2 K-1 on all vertical edges of the pouch almost impossible.

claims.claim: The heat released during full decomposition of the positive electrode is 448 J/g for Jiang and Dahn kinetics versus 145 J/g for Joachin et al. kinetics.
claims.quote: The heat released during full decomposition of the positive electrode is 448 J g-1 and 145 J g-1 for Jiang and Dahn [23] and Joachin et al. [22] kinetics, respectively.

claims.claim: For the Jiang and Dahn chemistry, thermal runaway only needs to be triggered at the positive electrode for the negative electrode breakdown reactions to propagate.
claims.quote: However, this study shows that for the Jiang and Dahn [23] chemistry, thermal runaway only needs to be triggered at the positive electrode for the negative electrode breakdown reactions to propagate.

claims.claim: Complete electrode breakdown of both negative and positive electrodes occurs after 300 s and 200 s for initial temperatures of 40°C and 60°C, respectively, compared to only 85% negative electrode decomposition with an initial temperature of 20°C.
claims.quote: Complete electrode breakdown of both negative and positive electrodes occurs after 300 s and 200 s for initial temperatures of 40 °C and 60 °C, respectively. This compares with only 85% of the negative electrode decomposing with an initial temperature of 20 °C.

claims.claim: Batteries cycled at -5°C/1C reached end of life (80% SOH) after only 60 cycles, whereas batteries cycled at 25°C and 55°C reached end of life after 2820 and 1020 cycles, respectively.
claims.quote: the batteries cycled at -5ºC exhibited rapid degradation and reached EoL after only 60 cycles, whereas the batteries cycled at higher temperatures exhibited better performance, with the cycle numbers reaching 2820 and 1020 at 25ºC and 55ºC, respectively.

claims.claim: Lithium plating on the anode is identified as the key reason for deterioration of battery thermal runaway performance during aging.
claims.quote: Lithium plating turns out to be the key reason for the deterioration of battery thermal runaway performance during aging process.

claims.claim: The evolution of battery thermal runaway performance during aging mainly depends on changes in the thermal stability of the anode+electrolyte thermodynamic system, not the cathode.
claims.quote: the evolution of battery TR performance mainly depends on the changes in the thermal stability of the anode+electrolyte thermodynamic system, whereas the side reactions at the cathode have little impact.

claims.claim: For batteries cycled at -5°C/1C, T1 decreased from 72.39°C to 52.40°C and T2 dropped by 23°C (from 213.3°C to 190.2°C) as SOH dropped from 100% to 77.5%, while ∆tTR decreased by 70% at 80% SOH.
claims.quote: T1 decreased from 72.39oC to 52.40oC when the SOH dropping from 100% to 77.5%. T2 also dropped by 23oC (from 213.3 oC to 190.2 oC) after cycling at -5ºC/1C, while T3 remained constant at different SOH. ∆tTR became much shorter as the batteries degraded and decreased by 70% when SOH reached 80%.

claims.claim: Batteries stored at 55°C/100% SOC showed improved thermal runaway performance, with T1 rising to 96.3°C and ∆tTR reaching 2.85 times its initial value, attributed to lower lithiation degree of the anode after high-temperature storage.
claims.quote: Batteries stored at 55ºC/100%SOC showed improved TR performance, with T1 rising to 96.3oC and ∆tTR reaching 2.85 times its initial value. According to Sec.3.2, the anodes had lower lithiation degree after high-temperature storage, resulting in the delayed onset temperature and reduced heat generation in the early stage of the battery TR process.

claims.claim: For batteries cycled at -5°C/1C, a new exothermic peak between 100°C and 180°C appeared in DSC profiles of the anode+electrolyte sample, with heat generation of 280.1 J/g from this peak (11.4% of total 2457.0 J/g), caused by reaction between electrolyte and plated lithium.
claims.quote: a new exothermic peak between 100 oC and 180oC appeared in the DSC profile of sample anode+electrolyte... The heat generation of the new exothermic peak reached 280.1 J·g-1, accounting for 11.4% of the total heat generation (2457.0 J·g-1). The exothermic peak was caused by the reaction between electrolyte and plated lithium on the anode surface.

claims.claim: Electrolyte consumption in batteries cycled at 25°C/2C caused a significant decrease of T3 from 829.3°C to 508.3°C at 78.5% SOH by reducing heat generation from reactions between the anode and electrolyte.
claims.quote: T3 decreased significantly to 508.3oC from 829.3oC when the battery SOH reached 78.5%, presenting much smaller heat generation during TR process... The decline of T3 after cycling at 25ºC/2C was benefit from electrolyte consumption, which reduced the heat generation of the reactions between the anode and electrolyte.

claims.claim: All fresh and degraded cathodes exhibited similar DSC profiles and heat generation, indicating little change in thermal stability of the cathode during the aging process.
claims.quote: all the fresh and degraded cathodes exhibited similar DSC profiles and heat generation, indicating little changes in the thermal stability of the cathode during aging process.

claims.claim: SEI film thickening reduces the lithiation degree of the fully-charged anode, resulting in improved battery thermal runaway performance with increased T1 and lower temperature rate.
claims.quote: SEI film thickening consumes lithium inventory, thus reducing the lithiation degree of the anode. The anode with a lower lithiation degree is more thermal stable and leads to improved battery TR performance with increased T1 and a lower temperature rate.

claims.claim: Safety accidents of electric vehicles in China typically occurred in vehicles after around one year of operation, according to the Lithium-ion Power Battery Safety Research Report (2019).
claims.quote: According to the Lithium-ion Power Battery Safety Research Report (2019) by the Battery Safety Laboratory at Tsinghua University, safety accidents of EVs in China usually happened in the vehicles after around one year operation.
